data.history:
The Al Nuaimi dynasty has ruled Ajman since the early 19th century, with Sheikh Rashid bin Humaid Al Nuaimi serving as the first recorded ruler from 1816-1838. The family belongs to the Qartasi house of Na'im tribe, which governs the emirate of Ajman as one of the seven ruling families of the United Arab Emirates. The dynasty has maintained continuous rule through successive generations, with power typically passing from father to son or between brothers within the family lineage.

Throughout the 19th and early 20th centuries, the Al Nuaimi rulers navigated the complex political landscape of the Trucial States period, when the region was under British protection. Like other ruling families in the region, they adapted to the transition from the traditional tribal system to modern state governance. The family follows the Hanbali school of Islamic jurisprudence, along with the other northern emirates of Sharjah, Umm al-Quwain, and Ras al-Khaimah.

The current ruler, Sheikh Humaid bin Rashid Al Nuaimi III, has governed Ajman since 1981, making him one of the longest-serving rulers among the UAE's seven emirates. Under his leadership, Ajman has participated in the federal structure of the United Arab Emirates while maintaining its distinct identity as the smallest emirate by area. The Al Nuaimi family continues to play a role in the UAE's Federal Supreme Council, which consists of the rulers of all seven emirates.
data.description: The Al Nuaimi are the ruling family of Ajman emirate in the UAE, belonging to the Qartasi house of Na'im tribe and following the Hanbali school of Islamic jurisprudence.
data.originStory: The Al Nuaimi belong to the Qartasi house of Na'im tribe, with their rule over Ajman beginning in the early 19th century under Sheikh Rashid bin Humaid Al Nuaimi.
data.modernStatus: Ruling family of Ajman emirate, one of the seven emirates of the United Arab Emirates, with Sheikh Humaid bin Rashid Al Nuaimi III serving as the current ruler since 1981.
data.nameEtymology: Nuaimi derives from the Na'im tribal name, as noted in the research that many modern Emirati names are derived from tribal names, such as 'Nuaimi (from Naim)'.

data.history: The Ba 'Alawi are a group of Hadhrami Sayyid families originating in Hadhramaut in the southwest corner of the Arabian Peninsula. They trace their lineage to Ahmad al-Muhajir, a descendant of Muhammad, who emigrated from Basra to Hadhramaut in 931 CE (318H) to avoid the chaos prevalent in the Abbasid Caliphate where descendants of Muhammad were continuously suspected of revolt. The name derives from Alawi bin 'Ubaydillah bin Ahmad al-Muhajir, the first of al-Muhajir's descendants to be named 'Alawi and the first to be born in Hadhramaut. In 521 AH, Ali bin Alawi moved to the city of Tarim, which became the headquarters of the Bani Alawi. The Ba 'Alawi developed their own Sufi order founded by Muhammad bin Ali Baalawi (Al-Faqih Al-Muqaddam), who symbolically broke his sword and renounced violence, establishing non-violence as a core principle. From 1700 AD onward, they began migrating in large numbers across the globe for Islamic missionary work (da'wah), spreading to Southeast Asia, East Africa, and other regions. Their genealogies have been preserved in 15 volumes compiled by Abd al-Rahman al-Mashhur and are maintained by institutions like the Al-Rabithah Al-Alawiyah in Jakarta. They follow the Shafi'i school in jurisprudence and Ash'ari school in faith, and are known for establishing mosques, religious schools, and advancing Islamic knowledge throughout their diaspora communities.
data.description: The Ba 'Alawi are Hadhrami Sayyid families who trace their lineage to Ahmad al-Muhajir, a descendant of Muhammad who emigrated from Basra to Hadhramaut in 931 CE. They are known for their global diaspora spreading Islamic teachings and their distinctive non-violent Sufi tradition.
data.originStory: Ahmad al-Muhajir, a descendant of Muhammad through the line of Husayn ibn Ali, emigrated from Basra, Iraq to Hadhramaut, Yemen in 931 CE (318H) to escape persecution and chaos in the Abbasid Caliphate where descendants of Muhammad were viewed with suspicion. His grandson Alawi was the first Sayyid to be born in Hadhramaut and the only one to produce a continued lineage there, giving the family its name Ba 'Alawi (descendants of Alawi).
data.modernStatus: The Ba 'Alawi maintain significant presence in Yemen, Indonesia, Malaysia, and other parts of the Muslim world. They operate educational institutions, maintain genealogical records through organizations like Rabithah Alawiyah in Indonesia, and continue their tradition of Islamic scholarship and Sufi teachings. Many have integrated into local societies while preserving their religious and cultural identity.
data.nameEtymology: Ba 'Alawi derives from Alawi bin 'Ubaydillah bin Ahmad al-Muhajir, the first descendant of Ahmad al-Muhajir to be named 'Alawi and the first to be born in Hadhramaut. The prefix 'Ba' in Hadhrami usage means 'children of' or 'descendants of'.

data.history: The Al Sulaim (Al-Sulaim) family is a royal dynasty that has ruled the city of Unaizah in Saudi Arabia's Al-Qassim Province since 1817. The dynasty was established when Prince Yahia bin Sulaiman Al Zamil Al-Sulaim killed the Ottoman-appointed governor and took control of Unaizah, ruling from 1817 until his death in 1836 at the Battle of Bag'a against the Al Rashid forces of Ha'il. The family originates from the Subay tribe, distinguishing them from other ruling families in the region like the Al Abu Olayan dynasty from Banu Tamim who ruled Buraydah. Prince Zamil al-Sulaim (1864-1887) became one of the most prominent leaders of his era, earning the nickname 'Alexander of the desert' among Bedouin tribes for his warfare skills. During his reign, the famous explorer Charles Montagu Doughty visited Unaizah and documented the city in his 1888 work 'Travels in Arabia Deserta'. The dynasty's most significant political achievement came under Prince Abdulaziz Abdullah al-Sulaim (1901-1914), who recaptured Unaizah after it was lost following the al-Mulaida war and signed a crucial treaty with King Abdulaziz that granted Al-Sulaim total sovereignty over Unaizah. This treaty formalized their autonomous rule within the emerging Saudi state, allowing them to maintain traditional governance while being incorporated into the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia.
data.description: The Al Sulaim dynasty has ruled the city of Unaizah in Saudi Arabia since 1817, originating from the Subay tribe. They maintain autonomous governance through a treaty with the Saudi royal family that grants them sovereignty over their city.
data.originStory: Prince Yahia bin Sulaiman Al Zamil Al-Sulaim established the dynasty in 1817 by killing the Ottoman-appointed governor of Unaizah and taking control of the city, founding a ruling house that would govern the settlement for over two centuries.
data.modernStatus: The Al Sulaim dynasty continues to rule Unaizah in accordance with a written treaty between them and the Saudi royal family, maintaining local sovereignty while being part of the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia.

data.history: The Al ash-Sheikh family are the descendants of Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab al-Tamimi al-Najdi (1703-1792), the 18th century founder of the Wahhabi sect who was born in the Nejd region. In the 1740s, Ibn Abd al-Wahhab moved to Diriyah in Nejd, where Muhammad ibn Saud was the local ruler. In 1744, the two leaders concluded a formal agreement where Ibn Saud declared his protection and support for Ibn Abd al-Wahhab's religious mission, while Ibn Abd al-Wahhab agreed to support Ibn Saud as political leader. This pact became a mutual support arrangement that has remained in place for nearly 300 years, with the Al Saud maintaining the Al ash-Sheikh's religious authority while the Al ash-Sheikh legitimize the Al Saud's political rule. The family suffered major setbacks in 1818 when Ottoman forces executed many family members including Sulayman ibn Abd Allah, and a whole branch was exiled to Egypt. However, they survived and continued to lead the Saudi religious establishment through the Second Saudi State from 1824 and into the modern Kingdom of Saudi Arabia established in 1932. Today, the Al ash-Sheikh remain Saudi Arabia's leading religious family, second in prestige only to the Al Saud royal family, holding key religious positions and maintaining extensive intermarriage with the royal family.
data.description: The Al ash-Sheikh are Saudi Arabia's leading religious family, descendants of Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab, founder of Wahhabism. They maintain a 300-year power-sharing pact with the Al Saud royal family, providing religious legitimacy in exchange for political protection.
data.originStory: The Al ash-Sheikh are the descendants of Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab al-Tamimi al-Najdi, born in 1703 in the Nejd region. He became influenced by the teachings of Ibn Taymiya and began preaching a puritanical form of Islam. In the 1740s, he moved to Diriyah where he formed his historic alliance with Muhammad ibn Saud in 1744.
data.modernStatus: Currently hold three ministerial positions in Saudi government as of 2020: Minister of Islamic Affairs (Saleh bin Abdul-Aziz Al ash-Sheikh), Minister of Municipal and Rural Affairs (Abdullatif bin Abdulmalik bin Omar Al-ash Sheikh), and Minister of Entertainment (Turki Al-Sheikh). Family members continue to dominate key religious institutions including the Senior Council of Ulema and serve as judges and other religious figures.
data.nameEtymology: The Arabic name Al ash-Sheikh (آل الشيخ) translates as 'family of the Sheikh' or 'House of the Sheikh.' The term ash-Sheikh refers to Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab, who was known as 'the Sheikh,' a term of respect for a noted elder, teacher or religious leader.

data.history: The Al Humaidan family has produced notable intelligence officials in modern Saudi Arabia. The most prominent member is Khalid bin Ali Al Humaidan, born in 1955 in Ha'il in northern Saudi Arabia. After studying criminal justice at Saginaw Valley State University in the United States, he enrolled in the Saudi Military in 1982 and served for over 20 years before entering intelligence work. He was appointed deputy head of the criminal investigation division in 2011 and promoted to lieutenant general. In January 2015, King Salman appointed him as director general of the General Intelligence Directorate through royal decree, replacing Youssef bin Ali Al Idrissi. He was also made a member of Saudi Arabia's Political and Security Affairs Council. The family appears to be part of the professional military and intelligence establishment that emerged in modern Saudi Arabia rather than having ancient tribal origins.
data.description: A Saudi family from the Ha'il region that has produced senior intelligence officials, most notably Khalid bin Ali Al Humaidan who has served as director general of the General Intelligence Directorate since 2015.
data.modernStatus: The family holds high-ranking positions in Saudi Arabia's intelligence apparatus, with Khalid bin Ali Al Humaidan serving as director general of the General Intelligence Directorate and member of the Political and Security Affairs Council since 2015.

data.history: The Al Jubeir family originates from the city of Majma'ah in the Riyadh region of Saudi Arabia. According to Arabic sources, they are part of the Al Mutarfi branch of the Hudhayl tribe. The family's full lineage is documented as 'Al Jubeir Al Mutarfi Al Hudhayli,' indicating their descent from the historic Hudhayl tribal confederation. The family has produced several prominent figures in Saudi government and academia, most notably Adel Al-Jubeir, who has served in various high-level diplomatic and ministerial positions. Adel's father, Ahmed Mohammed Al-Jubeir, worked in the cultural attaché office at the Saudi embassy in Germany, establishing an early connection to international diplomacy. The family also includes Sheikh Mohammed bin Ibrahim bin Jubeir, Adel's uncle, who held various ministerial positions including head of the Board of Grievances, Minister of Justice, and Chairman of the Shura Council. This demonstrates the family's long-standing involvement in Saudi governmental and judicial affairs, with multiple generations serving in senior positions within the Kingdom's administrative structure.
data.description: The Al Jubeir family is a prominent Saudi family from Majma'ah, part of the Al Mutarfi branch of the Hudhayl tribe, known for producing senior diplomats and government officials including Foreign Minister Adel Al-Jubeir.
data.originStory: The Al Jubeir family traces its origins to Majma'ah city in the Riyadh region, belonging to the Al Mutarfi branch of the ancient Hudhayl tribe. The family name follows the traditional Arabian genealogical pattern as 'Al Jubeir Al Mutarfi Al Hudhayli.'
data.modernStatus: The family holds prominent positions in Saudi government and diplomacy. Adel Al-Jubeir currently serves as Minister of State for Foreign Affairs and previously served as Foreign Minister (2015-2018) and Ambassador to the United States (2007-2015). The family maintains significant influence in Saudi foreign policy and international relations.

data.history: The Al Nahedh family appears to be a prominent business family in the Gulf region, particularly connected to the financial sector. The research reveals that Mazin Al Nahedh served as former CEO of Kuwait Finance House (KFH), one of the most significant Islamic financial institutions in the region, established in 1977 as Kuwait's first Islamic bank. KFH has grown to become a major player in Islamic banking with assets totaling $55.52 billion and operations across Kuwait, Bahrain, Saudi Arabia, Turkey, and Malaysia. The family name also appears in Saudi Arabia, where Hani Al-Nahedh (born 1987) is a professional footballer who has played for various clubs including Al-Ittihad, Al-Riyadh, and Al-Saqer, suggesting the family may have branches or connections across the Gulf states.
data.description: Al Nahedh is a Gulf family prominently involved in Islamic finance and business, with notable figures including Mazin Al Nahedh, former CEO of Kuwait Finance House, and presence in both Kuwait and Saudi Arabia.
data.modernStatus: The Al Nahedh family holds significant positions in Gulf business and finance, particularly in Islamic banking through Kuwait Finance House, with family members also active in sports and other sectors across Kuwait and Saudi Arabia.

data.history: The research provided contains no information about the Al Quraishi family as a Gulf genealogical entity. The Wikipedia entry about Abu Ibrahim al-Hashimi al-Qurashi refers to an ISIS leader who used 'al-Qurashi' as a nom de guerre, claiming descent from the Quraysh tribe of Muhammad for religious legitimacy purposes. His real name was Amir Mohammed Abdul Rahman al-Mawli al-Salbi, born in Iraq in 1976. The research on Al Sadd SC discusses a Qatari football club with no connection to any Al Quraishi family. The marriage in Islam article provides general Islamic law context but no specific family information. No verified historical, genealogical, or business information about an Al Quraishi family in the GCC context is present in the provided research materials.

data.history: The Fitaihi family appears to be a prominent Saudi Arabian family with business and professional backgrounds. Based on the available research, Walid Fitaihi was born in Jeddah in 1964 and pursued medical studies and work in the United States for two decades before returning to Saudi Arabia in 2006. The family has connections to other notable Saudi families, as evidenced by Adel Fakeih's marriage to Maha Fitaihi, described as 'a leading businesswoman and social figure.' Walid Fitaihi's case gained international attention when he was arrested at the Ritz-Carlton hotel in November 2017 during the Saudi government's anti-corruption purge and was subsequently moved to Al-Ha'ir Prison south of Riyadh, where he reportedly faced torture including electric shocks and severe whipping. The family appears to have established itself in Jeddah, with connections to both the medical profession and business sectors in Saudi Arabia.
data.description: A Saudi Arabian family with documented presence in Jeddah, known for professional achievements in medicine and business, with connections to prominent Saudi business circles.
data.modernStatus: The family has established itself in professional and business circles in Saudi Arabia, with documented connections to prominent businesswomen and involvement in medical practice, though some members have faced political difficulties.

data.history: Based on the available research, the Al Balooshi family has produced notable political and athletic figures in the Gulf region. The most prominent documented member is Fatima Mohammed Al Balooshi, a Bahraini politician who served in multiple ministerial positions including Social Development, Human Rights, and Acting Health Minister in the Bahraini Cabinet. During the 2011 Bahraini protests, she led the government's response regarding medical personnel and detained protesters, making controversial statements that were criticized by international observers including Irish parliamentary delegations. Her political prominence was recognized by Forbes Middle East, which included her on their list of the 200 most powerful Arab women in government. The family has also produced athletes, with Aisha Al Balooshi representing the UAE in weightlifting at international competitions including the 2016 Rio Olympics, though her career was marred by a doping violation that resulted in the stripping of her 2011 Arab Games silver medal.
data.description: A Gulf family with documented members in Bahraini politics and Emirati athletics, most notably through politician Fatima Mohammed Al Balooshi and Olympic weightlifter Aisha Al Balooshi.
data.modernStatus: The family has maintained political influence in Bahrain through Fatima Mohammed Al Balooshi's ministerial roles and athletic representation through Aisha Al Balooshi's Olympic participation for the UAE.

She finished 16th at the 2016 Rio Olympics and won silver at the 2011 Arab Games, though was later stripped of the medal due to performance-enhancing drug use.

data.history: The Al Otaiba family traces its modern prominence to Mana Saeed Al Otaiba, who served as the UAE's first Minister of Petroleum and was one of the country's key non-royal founding members. As a close confidant to UAE founder and President Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan (1918–2004), Mana Al Otaiba played a crucial role in the early formation of the United Arab Emirates and served as president of OPEC a record six times. The family established the Al Otaiba Group, which operated in the automotive sector, including holding the General Motors/Cadillac agency in Abu Dhabi, though they later lost this dealership after an 11-year dispute over non-performance. The family's influence expanded significantly through Mana's son Yousef Al Otaiba, born in Egypt in 1974 to an Egyptian mother (one of Mana's four wives). Yousef became a key advisor to Mohammed bin Zayed Al Nahyan from age 26, serving as director of international affairs and the country's principal security, anti-terrorism and defense liaison. In 2008, at age 34, he was appointed UAE ambassador to the United States, becoming one of the most influential diplomats in Washington and playing crucial roles in major regional developments including the Abraham Accords normalization with Israel in 2020.
data.description: A prominent UAE family led by petroleum magnate Mana Saeed Al Otaiba, founding member of the UAE, with his son Yousef serving as influential UAE Ambassador to the United States since 2008.
data.modernStatus: Major political and business family with Yousef Al Otaiba serving as UAE Ambassador to the US and Minister of State since 2008, promoted to ministerial rank in 2017 while maintaining ambassadorial role. The family operates the Al Otaiba Group business conglomerate.

data.history: Al-Awadhi (العوضي) is an Arabic surname with notable presence across the Gulf Cooperation Council countries, particularly in Kuwait, Qatar, Bahrain, and the UAE. The family has produced significant political figures, entertainers, and professionals throughout the region. In Kuwait specifically, the Al-Awadhi family has been active in politics and public service, with members serving in the National Assembly and holding ministerial positions. The family includes both Sunni and Shia members, reflecting the diverse sectarian composition within Kuwaiti society. Modern Al-Awadhi family members have achieved prominence in various fields including politics, entertainment, journalism, sports, and business across multiple Gulf states.

data.modernStatus: The Al-Awadhi family maintains significant presence in Gulf politics and society, with members serving as politicians, entertainers, journalists, athletes, and business professionals across Kuwait, Qatar, Bahrain, and UAE

data.history: The Al Brahim family name derives from Ibrahim, the Arabic form of Abraham, which is common among Muslims and Arab Christians throughout the Middle East. According to Wikipedia sources, Ibrahim as a surname can indicate Jewish origin, particularly among Mizrahi populations in Arabic-speaking countries prior to their exodus to Israel. The surname was also used as an anglicization of the Arabic Ibrahim by families settling in various regions. As noted in the research, 'Abraham has also been used as an anglicisation of the equivalent Arabic surname Ibrāhīm.' The name appears across the GCC region among various communities, though the specific origins and migration patterns of individual Al Brahim families would require additional genealogical research to establish definitively.
data.description: Al Brahim is a family name derived from Ibrahim, the Arabic form of Abraham, found throughout the Gulf region among Muslim, Christian, and historically Jewish communities.
data.nameEtymology: Derived from Ibrahim, the Arabic form of Abraham, from Hebrew Avraham meaning 'father of a multitude of nations.' In the Levant and Maghreb, Brahim is a common diminutive for the first name Ibrahim.

data.history: The Zainal name appears across multiple distinct family lines with varying origins and geographic distributions. The most prominent documented branch is the Malaysian royal House of Zainal Abidin, descended from Sultan Mahmud Al-Muktafi Billah Shah of Terengganu. Sultan Mizan Zainal Abidin, born in 1962 as the eldest son of Sultan Mahmud, has ruled Terengganu since 1998 and served as Malaysia's King from 2006-2011. His mother, Fatimah Sharifah Nong Alsagoff, came from an Arab family of Sayid Omar Aljunied lineage, connecting the family to Singapore's early Arab pioneers. A separate Australian branch exists with Farzian Zainal, born on Christmas Island to a Malay family whose grandfather migrated from British Malaya in the 1950s to work in the phosphate industry. The Saudi Arabian House of Alireza, founded by Zainal Alireza in 1845 after arriving in Jeddah from Laristan in southern Iran, represents another distinct lineage that became one of Saudi Arabia's oldest commercial enterprises and early backers of King Abdulaziz al Saud.
data.description: Zainal represents multiple distinct family lineages including Malaysian royalty (House of Zainal Abidin of Terengganu), Australian-Malay families from Christmas Island, and the founding patriarch of Saudi Arabia's House of Alireza commercial dynasty.
data.originStory: Multiple distinct origin stories exist: the Malaysian royal line traces to Terengganu sultanate with Arab maternal lineage from Singapore; the Australian branch originates from British Malayan workers who migrated to Christmas Island in the 1950s; the Saudi Alireza line founded by Zainal Alireza who arrived in Jeddah from Laristan, southern Iran in 1845.
data.modernStatus: The Malaysian Zainal Abidin line maintains royal status as ruling Sultan of Terengganu; the Australian branch holds political office with Farzian Zainal serving as Administrator of Australian Indian Ocean Territories since 2023; the Saudi Alireza branch continues as major commercial enterprise

data.history: The Dadabhai family refers to the lineage of Dadabhai Naoroji (1825-1917), a prominent Gujarati Parsi political leader, merchant, and scholar who played a pivotal role in both Indian and British public life. Born in Navsari in a Gujarati-speaking Parsi Zoroastrian family, Naoroji was educated at the Elphinstone Institute School and began his career as Dewan to the Maharaja of Baroda in 1874. As an ordained Athornan priest, he founded the Rahnumai Mazdayasan Sabha in 1851 to restore Zoroastrian religion to its original purity. His early career included serving as Professor of Mathematics and Natural Philosophy at Elphinstone College in Bombay in 1855, becoming the first Indian to hold such an academic position. In business, he partnered with Cama & Co in London and Liverpool, establishing the first Indian company in Britain, before founding his own cotton trading company, Dadabhai Naoroji & Co, in 1859. Naoroji moved between India and Britain throughout his career, founding the London Indian Society in 1865 and the East India Association in 1867. He became a founding member of the Indian National Congress and served as its President three times (1886-1887, 1893-1894, 1906-1907). In 1892, he was elected to the House of Commons as a Liberal Party MP for Finsbury Central, becoming the first British Indian Member of Parliament. His granddaughters Perin and Khurshedben continued the family's involvement in the independence movement, with Khurshedben being arrested in 1930 for attempting to hoist the Indian flag at a Government College in Ahmedabad.
data.description: The Dadabhai family descends from Dadabhai Naoroji, a pioneering Gujarati Parsi political leader, merchant, and scholar who became the first Indian Member of the British Parliament and was instrumental in founding the Indian National Congress.
data.originStory: The family originates from Navsari in Gujarat, India, where Dadabhai Naoroji was born into a Gujarati-speaking Parsi Zoroastrian family on 4 September 1825.
data.modernStatus: The family's legacy continues through commemorative recognition, including the British government's Dadabhai Naoroji Awards launched in 2014 for contributions to UK-India relations, and postal stamps issued by India Post in 1963, 1997, and 2017.

data.history: The Al Harthi family represents multiple distinct lineages across the Arabian Peninsula and Gulf region, with the most documented branch being the Banu al-Harith tribe of Yemen. The Yemeni Al-Harthi are established in Shabwah and Marib governorates, with members like Qaed Salim Sinan al-Harethi (1955-2002) born in Shabwah who later moved to Marib due to South Yemen's hostility toward tribal customs in the 1970s. In Oman, the Al-Harthi family has produced prominent political figures, including Sheikh Hamoud bin Abdullah al-Harthi (c.1940-2004) from the Hirth tribe in al-Sharqiyah region, who served as Minister of Justice and Chairman of the Council of State. The family also includes contemporary figures like author Jokha Alharthi (born 1978), the first Arab to win the Man Booker International Prize in 2019. Sports figures include Saudi footballer Saad Al-Harthi and Omani sprinter Barakat Al-Harthi (born 1988), demonstrating the family's geographic spread across multiple Gulf states and their involvement in various professional fields from politics to literature to athletics.
data.description: Al Harthi is a widespread Arabian family name found across Yemen, Oman, and Saudi Arabia, with documented lineages including the Yemeni Banu al-Harith tribe and Omani Hirth tribe members who have achieved prominence in politics, literature, and sports.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains significant presence across multiple Gulf states with members in government (Omani politics), academia (Sultan Qaboos University), literature (international award winners), and professional sports, representing diverse professional achievements rather than concentrated business interests.

data.history: The Al Riyami family is established in the Ash Sharqiyah Region of Oman, particularly in the Wilayat of Ibra and surrounding villages. In the village of Thabti in Upper Ibra, Khalifa bin Hamood Al Riyami serves as the sheikh leading the Al Riyami families according to Oman's 2010 national census. The family has significant presence in Ibra, which is described as home to several major Omani tribes including Al Riyami, Al Maskari, Al Harthy, Al Mughairi, and Al Ismaili. The Al Riyami family has produced notable political figures in modern Oman, with Rahila bint Amur Al Riyami becoming one of the first two women directly elected to Oman's Consultative Assembly in 2000, representing Bawshar constituency. She served as Director of Educational Planning at the Ministry of Education for 16 years before her political career, was re-elected in 2003, and later appointed to the Council of State in 2007 where she headed Human Resources Development until her death in 2017.
data.description: The Al Riyami is an established Omani family with significant presence in the Ash Sharqiyah Region, particularly Ibra, led by tribal sheikhs and producing notable political figures including pioneering female parliamentarian Rahila Al Riyami.
data.modernStatus: The Al Riyami maintain traditional tribal leadership in the Ash Sharqiyah Region with Khalifa bin Hamood Al Riyami serving as sheikh of Al Riyami families in Thabti village, while the family has achieved political prominence through figures like Rahila Al Riyami who broke barriers as one of Oman's first elected female parliamentarians.

data.history: The Al Mukhaini family is documented as having origins traced to the Sur city in Oman, as evidenced by the genealogy of prominent footballer Ismail Matar Ibrahim Khamis Al Mukhaini Al Junaibi. The family demonstrates connections across the Gulf region, with documented relationships to other prominent Gulf families. Ismail Matar's personal background reveals that the Al Mukhaini family has established itself in the UAE, where family members have achieved prominence in professional sports. The family maintains close relationships with other Gulf families, as evidenced by Ismail Matar's cousin relationship with Ahmed Hadid, a midfielder for the Oman national football team and Al Ittihad player. Multiple family members have pursued careers in professional football, indicating a family tradition in athletics.
data.description: A Gulf family with verified origins in Sur, Oman, now established in the UAE with notable achievements in professional football and documented connections to other prominent Gulf sporting families.
data.originStory: The Al Mukhaini family's origins can be traced from the Sur city in Oman, as documented in the genealogy of family member Ismail Matar Ibrahim Khamis Al Mukhaini Al Junaibi.

data.history: The Al Bassam family name derives from the Arabic root "ba-sa-ma" meaning "to smile," with Bassam being the exaggerated form meaning "one who smiles profusely." The name is most common in the Levant region. Among notable individuals with this surname is Sulayman Al-Bassam, born in 1972, an Anglo-Kuwaiti playwright who represents a modern cultural figure bearing this name. The research indicates the name has historical roots, with Ibn Bassam (1058-1147) being a notable Moorish writer from the medieval period. However, the specific research provided does not contain detailed information about the Al Bassam family's origins, migration patterns, or establishment in the Gulf region, focusing instead on the etymology and notable individuals with the Bassam name across different regions and time periods.
data.description: An Arabic family name meaning "one who smiles profusely," most commonly found in the Levant, with notable figures including medieval scholars and modern cultural personalities.
data.nameEtymology: Derives from Arabic root "ba-sa-ma" meaning "to smile." Bassam is the exaggerated form of the agent noun, roughly translating to "one who smiles profusely." The name is most common in the Levant region.

data.history: The Al Essa family represents multiple distinct lineages across the Arab world, with no confirmed common ancestry between the various branches. The Syrian branch, exemplified by poet Sulaiman al-Issa (1921-2013), originated from Al-Nairiyah village near Antioch. His father Sheikh Ahmad al-Issa established a kuttab (traditional Islamic school) where Sulaiman received his early education. The family fled to Syria in 1939 after Turkey's annexation of the Sanjak of Alexandretta, settling in various Syrian cities including Hama, Latakia, and Damascus. The Qatari branch appears in modern sports through the Barsham family, where Mutaz Essa Barsham became an Olympic champion high jumper, with his brother Meshaal Essa Barsham serving as a professional goalkeeper. The family name also appears in Gulf business contexts, as seen with Raja Easa Al Gurg in Dubai's business sector, though no verified connections exist between these different Al Essa branches across the region.
data.description: Al Essa represents multiple unrelated family lineages across Syria, Qatar, and the Gulf region, with notable figures in literature, sports, and business. No confirmed genealogical connections exist between the various branches.
data.modernStatus: The Syrian Al Essa branch gained prominence through literary contributions and political involvement with the Ba'ath Party. The Qatari branch has achieved international recognition in athletics and football, with family members competing at Olympic and professional levels.

migrationPath.description: Sulaiman al-Issa's family fled after Turkish annexation of Sanjak of Alexandretta

data.history: Prince Al Waleed bin Talal Al Saud was born on 7 March 1955 in Jeddah to Prince Talal bin Abdulaziz and Mona El Solh. He is a grandson of King Abdulaziz, the founder of Saudi Arabia, and of Riad Al Solh, Lebanon's first prime minister. His paternal grandmother was an Armenian woman, Munaiyir, who was presented to King Abdulaziz in 1921. After his parents separated when he was seven, Al Waleed lived with his mother in Lebanon and attended schools there and in the United States, receiving a bachelor's degree from Menlo College in 1979 and a master's degree from Syracuse University in 1985. He began his business career in 1979 with $30,000 from his father, forming Kingdom Establishment in 1980. His breakthrough came with strategic investments in distressed Western companies, most notably Citicorp in 1991 where he invested $590 million when the bank was struggling. By the 1990s, he had built a global investment empire spanning hotels, media, and technology companies, becoming known as the 'Arabian Warren Buffett.' In November 2017, he was arrested along with other Saudi elites in Mohammed bin Salman's anti-corruption purge and held at the Ritz-Carlton Riyadh for nearly three months before his release in January 2018 following a reported $6 billion settlement.
data.description: Al Waleed bin Talal is a Saudi billionaire businessman and member of the House of Saud royal family, founder of Kingdom Holding Company, known for strategic investments in distressed Western assets including Citicorp, hotels, and media companies.
data.originStory: The family traces to King Abdulaziz (Ibn Saud), the founder of Saudi Arabia, through Prince Talal bin Abdulaziz. Al Waleed's paternal grandmother Munaiyir was an Armenian woman whose family escaped from the Ottoman Empire between 1915 and 1923, presented to King Abdulaziz by the emir of Unayza in 1921 when she was 12 years old. His maternal lineage connects to Lebanese political leadership through Riad Al Solh, Lebanon's first prime minister.
data.modernStatus: Al Waleed owns 95% of Kingdom Holding Company, a Saudi conglomerate with investments in hotels (Four Seasons, Plaza Hotel), media companies, and real estate. His net worth peaked at $39.8 billion in 2017 before his arrest and settlement with the Saudi government. He continues to operate from Saudi Arabia with reduced influence following his 2017-2018 detention.

data.history:
The research reveals multiple distinct Al Mutlaq entities with different origins and contexts. The most prominent is the religious office of Da'i al-Mutlaq, the highest spiritual rank in Tayyibi Ismaili Islam, established in 528 AH/1134 AD when the 21st Tayyibi Imam went into occultation. Queen Arwa al-Sulayhi in Yemen appointed Syedna Zoeb bin Moosa as the first Da'i al-Mutlaq under instructions from the 20th Imam al-Amir. This religious institution has continued through multiple successor communities including Dawoodi Bohras, Alavi Bohras, and Sulaymanis, with various branches claiming legitimate succession.

In modern Iraqi politics, Saleh Muhammed al-Mutlaq represents a completely separate Al Mutlaq lineage. Born in Fallujah in 1947 into the Jubur Tribe, he graduated from the University of Baghdad in 1968 and completed his PhD at the University of Aberdeen in 1974. Originally a Ba'ath Party member, he was expelled in 1977 for criticizing the government. After the 2003 invasion of Iraq, he returned to politics as head of the Iraqi Front for National Dialogue and served as Deputy Prime Minister from 2010-2015.

The term 'al-Mutlaq' also appears in Shia Islamic religious hierarchy as 'Marja al-taqlid al-mutlaq' (the highest marja or religious authority), though this represents a religious title rather than a family name. The research provides no evidence connecting these various uses of 'al-Mutlaq' to a single family lineage.
data.description: Al Mutlaq appears in multiple contexts: as a religious title in Tayyibi Ismaili leadership, as an Iraqi political family from the Jubur Tribe, and as a designation for the highest Shia religious authority.
data.tribalOrigin: Jubur Tribe (for Iraqi Al Mutlaq branch only)
data.modernStatus: The Iraqi Al Mutlaq branch remains active in politics through Saleh al-Mutlaq's Iraqi Front for National Dialogue. The religious Da'i al-Mutlaq offices continue across multiple Ismaili communities worldwide with headquarters in Mumbai, Vadodara, Najran, and other locations.
data.nameEtymology: Al-Mutlaq means 'absolute, unrestricted' in Arabic, derived from the religious title Da'i al-Mutlaq in Tayyibi Ismaili Islam

data.history: The Al Arfaj family emerges in the research as part of the 'Al Aqilat' trading community that flourished in Al-Qassim Province, Saudi Arabia during the late 1850s and early 1900s. According to the research, they were among the most important families of the Aqilat, alongside Aba Alkhail, Al Rumaih, Al Sugair, Al Rebdi, and others. These families represented settled tribes from Najd, specifically from Al-Qassim, and became renowned traders dealing primarily in gold, Arabian horses, camels, clothing, and food across the Arabian Peninsula. The Al Arfaj and other Aqilat families established extensive trade networks reaching Kuwait, Iraq, and the Levant (modern Jordan and Syria). They were distinguished by their unique practice of wearing the Aqal and distinctive uniforms, becoming well-known throughout the Arabian Peninsula and Arab world for their courage, generosity, honesty, and patience during long-distance travels. The research indicates they played a crucial role in the economic development of the region and were recognized as the first ambassadors of King Abdul Aziz, with later Saudi leadership praising them as pioneers who brought trade to the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia.
data.description: The Al Arfaj family was one of the prominent trading families among the 'Al Aqilat' merchant community of Al-Qassim Province, Saudi Arabia, active in the late 19th and early 20th centuries. They were known for their extensive trade networks across the Arabian Peninsula and beyond.
data.originStory: The Al Arfaj family originated as part of settled tribes and families from Najd, specifically from Al-Qassim Province in Saudi Arabia, during the late 1850s and early 1900s period.
data.nameEtymology: The name 'Arfaj' is associated with Rhanterium epapposum, a desert plant known locally as 'arfaj' throughout the Arabian Peninsula, which is the national flower of both Kuwait and Saudi Arabia.

timelineEvents.title: Al Aqilat Trading Era Begins
timelineEvents.description: The Al Arfaj family becomes established as part of the Al Aqilat trading community in Al-Qassim Province

data.history: Based on the available research, Al Barrak appears as a surname associated with different individuals across the Middle East, but no unified family history is documented. The research shows Abdulrahman bin Abdullah Al Barrak serving as assistant chairman of Saudi Arabia's Consultative Assembly from February 2009 to December 2011, indicating a presence in Saudi governmental circles. Additionally, Abdul-Rahman al-Barrak is mentioned as a prominent Saudi cleric who issued controversial religious rulings. The name also appears in historical contexts, with Musallam Al-Barrak noted as a former Kuwaiti MP and opposition leader who was sentenced to prison in 2015 for criticizing the Emir. However, the research provides no evidence of these individuals being part of a single Al Barrak family lineage or any verified genealogical connections between them. The surname appears across different Gulf states but without documented family unity or shared origins.
data.description: Al Barrak is a surname appearing among various individuals in Saudi Arabia and Kuwait, including government officials and religious figures, though no unified family structure is documented in available research.
data.modernStatus: Present in Saudi governmental and religious circles, with individuals holding positions in the Consultative Assembly and religious establishment, though no coordinated family business or political structure is documented

data.history: Al-Bishi (Arabic: البيشي) is an Arabic family name with documented presence in Saudi Arabia. The surname appears to be well-established within Saudi society, with multiple family members achieving prominence in athletics and football during the late 20th and early 21st centuries. The research shows several individuals bearing this name who have distinguished themselves in sports, particularly football and athletics, suggesting the family has maintained a notable presence in Saudi sporting circles. One significant military figure, Brigadier General Abdel Rahman Al-Bishi, served as head of operations for the Rapid Support Forces in Sennar and Blue Nile States during the current Sudanese civil war, indicating the name's presence extends beyond Saudi Arabia to other Arab regions. However, the specific origins, migration patterns, and early history of the Al-Bishi family are not documented in the available research.
data.description: Al-Bishi is an Arabic family name documented primarily in Saudi Arabia, with notable family members in sports and military service.
data.modernStatus: The family has produced several notable athletes and sports figures in Saudi Arabia, as well as military personnel in regional conflicts.

data.history: The research provided does not contain any information about the Ba Harith family. The documents cover various historical topics including medieval Islamic figures like Abu al-Fadl al-Tamimi (a 10th-11th century Sufi saint from Yemen belonging to the al-Tamimi tribe), the ancient oasis of Khaybar in Saudi Arabia, Islamic religious figures like Muhammad al-Faqih al-Muqaddam (founder of the Ba 'Alawiyya Sufi order), cemetery history, genealogical information about Hashim ibn Abd Manaf and Banu Hashim, Sufi orders, and musical instrument history. However, none of these sources provide any direct information about a family called Ba Harith, their origins, migration patterns, business activities, or notable figures. Without specific research about the Ba Harith family, no verified historical narrative can be constructed.

data.history: Ba Hashwan is one of the Hadrami Arab families that migrated to Singapore as part of the broader Arab migration to Asia, Africa, and Europe for trade purposes. Like other Hadrami families, they were involved in bringing Islam and Arabic language to Singapore, establishing strong commercial networks particularly in oil and general trade businesses. During the British colonial period, Arab families including Ba Hashwan maintained close ties with their Hadramawt homeland, sending their children back to learn Arabic culture and strengthen their Arab identity. After World War II, wealthy Arab families began sending their children to London for education, while others sent them to Aden instead of Hadramawt. The 1960s marked a fundamental change when South Yemen gained independence with a communist government, effectively cutting off the return path to the original homeland. As Singapore's economic development prioritized English language and higher education, the new generation of Singaporean Arabs gradually lost their Arabic language and Hadrami identity.
data.description: Ba Hashwan is a Hadrami Arab family that settled in Singapore as part of the historical Arab migration for trade, contributing to the Islamic and Arabic cultural influence in the region.
data.originStory: Ba Hashwan originated from Hadramawt (modern-day Yemen) and migrated to Singapore as part of the Hadrami Arab commercial diaspora that spread across Asia for trade purposes.
data.modernStatus: Like other Arab families in Singapore, Ba Hashwan faces identity challenges as the current generation cannot speak Arabic and has lost connection to Hadramawt. The community has established Arabic language centers to help the rising generation learn their language, culture, and heritage.

migrationPath.description: Migration as part of Hadrami Arab commercial diaspora for trade purposes

timelineEvents.title: South Yemen Independence
timelineEvents.description: Independence of South Yemen with communist government cut off return path to Hadramawt homeland

connections.context: Ba Hashwan is part of the Hadrami Arab community from Hadramawt, Yemen

data.history:
The Al Baghdadi family referenced in this research primarily relates to Iraqi militant leaders rather than a traditional GCC merchant or tribal family. Abu Omar al-Baghdadi (born Hamid Dawud Mohamed Khalil al-Zawi) was born in 1964 in Al-Zawiyah village near Haditha in Al-Anbar Governorate, Iraq. He descended from the Qurayshi Al-Arajiyah tribe, with his father Muhammad Khalil al-Zawi serving as a prominent local sheikh. After graduating from Baghdad's Police Academy and working as a police officer in Haditha, he was dismissed in 1993 due to his Salafist ideology and later became involved in militant activities following the 2003 U.S. invasion of Iraq.

Abu Bakr al-Baghdadi (Ibrahim Awwad Ibrahim Ali al-Badri, 1971-2019) was born in Samarra to a Sunni Arab family belonging to the Al-Bu Badri tribe, which includes sub-tribes such as Radhawiyyah, Husseiniyyah, Adnaniyyah, and Quraysh. His father Awwad Ibrahim was a religious cleric, and his grandfather Ibrahim Ali al-Badri reportedly lived to age 94. Al-Baghdadi obtained graduate degrees in Islamic theology from the Islamic University of Baghdad in the late 1990s and 2000s, including reportedly a PhD in 2007.

Both individuals became leaders of Iraqi militant organizations - Abu Omar al-Baghdadi led the Islamic State of Iraq from 2006 until his death in 2010, succeeded by Abu Bakr al-Baghdadi who expanded the organization into Syria and declared himself caliph in 2014. Abu Bakr al-Baghdadi was killed in a U.S. military operation in Syria in 2019. This appears to be an Iraqi militant lineage rather than a traditional GCC business or tribal family.
data.description: Iraqi militant family lineage associated with Islamic State leadership, originating from Al-Anbar Governorate and Samarra, claiming descent from Qurayshi tribes.
data.originStory: The Al Baghdadi militant leaders originated from different Iraqi tribal backgrounds - Abu Omar from the Qurayshi Al-Arajiyah in Al-Anbar, and Abu Bakr from the Al-Bu Badri tribe in Samarra, both claiming Qurayshi descent.
data.tribalOrigin: Al-Bu Badri (for Abu Bakr al-Baghdadi), Qurayshi Al-Arajiyah (for Abu Omar al-Baghdadi)
data.modernStatus: The militant organization these individuals led was designated as a terrorist organization by the United Nations and most sovereign states. Both leaders were killed in military operations.
data.nameEtymology: Al-Baghdadi surname literally means 'The one from Baghdad' and denotes origin from Baghdad city or governorate in Iraq.

data.history: Based on the available research, the Al Buraida family name appears to be connected to the city of Buraida (also spelled Buraidah) in the Al-Qassim Province of Saudi Arabia. The research indicates that Buraida has been a significant urban center in central Arabia, particularly during the Ottoman-Wahhabi conflicts of the early 19th century. During the Third Campaign of the Ottoman-Wahhabi War (1816-1818), Ottoman-Egyptian forces under Ibrahim Pasha overran several towns in the Qasim and Najd regions, including Buraida, by 1817. The city was strategically important enough to be specifically mentioned alongside other major centers like Rass and Unayza. The research also shows historical connections between the Al-Hamid family and Buraida, as evidenced by Abdullah al-Hamid's imprisonment at al-Buraida prison. However, the research does not provide direct information about the Al Buraida family's specific origins, tribal affiliations, or genealogical history.
data.description: A family name associated with the historic city of Buraida in Saudi Arabia's Al-Qassim Province, with documented connections to the region's political and historical events.

timelineEvents.title: Ottoman Forces Capture Buraida
timelineEvents.description: During the Third Campaign of the Ottoman-Wahhabi War, Ibrahim Pasha's forces overran Buraida along with other towns in Qasim and Najd regions

connections.context: Abdullah al-Hamid was imprisoned at al-Buraida prison, showing family connections to the city

data.history: Al Ahsa refers to the historical region and governorate in the Eastern Province of Saudi Arabia, centered around the cities of Al-Hofuf and Al-Mubarraz. The region has deep historical significance, with Al-Mubarraz serving as the administrative center for the district between 1669 and 1793, before Saudi rule was established. The area contains ancient settlements with some foundations dating back four thousand years, though most current structures were built within the last three centuries. During Ibn Saud's campaigns to unite Arabia in the early 20th century, he reached Al Ahsa in December 1901 during his raiding expedition into Nejd, using it as a strategic point before proceeding south towards the Empty Quarter. The region completed its integration under Saudi control by 1912 when Ibn Saud finished his conquest of Nejd and the eastern coast of Arabia. Al Ahsa became notable for its springs, agricultural areas, and as home to various tribal families, including the Alsaad family in Al-Jeshah village, who are recognized as one of the largest and most respected families in their community.
data.description: Al Ahsa is a historic region in Saudi Arabia's Eastern Province, known for its ancient settlements, natural springs, and role in Ibn Saud's early 20th century unification campaigns.
data.modernStatus: Al Ahsa remains an important governorate in Saudi Arabia's Eastern Province, with Al-Mubarraz serving as a major city with a population of 837,000 as of 2020.

folkLegends.title: Origin of Al-Mubarraz Name
folkLegends.story: There are sayings that Al-Mubarraz got its name because people would gather there before leaving for Al-Hajj pilgrimage, making it a meeting point for travelers. Sheikh Muhammad Alabdulaqader wrote that the people of Al-Ahsa would gather there first before departing for Hajj.
folkLegends.source: Local tradition recorded by Sheikh Muhammad Alabdulaqader

data.nameEtymology: In Gulf Arabic, Al-Mubarraz stands for 'outstanding' or 'masterly.' In Nejdi Arabic, it refers to a place where travelers gathered before leaving on journeys.

data.history: The research provided does not contain any information about the Al Arar family's genealogy, business history, or tribal lineage. The Wikipedia articles focus entirely on terrorist incidents in Saudi Arabia and general information about Jeddah city, with no mention of the Al Arar family specifically. While the city of Arar is referenced in one terrorist incident from 2015 where suicide bombers attacked a border patrol unit near Arar, killing three soldiers, this geographic reference provides no information about a family bearing that name. Without direct textual evidence about the Al Arar family's origins, migration patterns, business activities, or tribal connections, no verified historical narrative can be constructed.

The research provided does not contain information about the Al Baha family as a genealogical entity. The primary content focuses on Al-Baha as a city and province in Saudi Arabia's Hejaz region, which is the homeland of the Ghamid and Zahran tribes. The province comprises 18 tribes branching from these two main tribal groups - 13 settled tribes engaged in trades and agriculture, and 5 nomadic tribes. The area has been historically significant as the administrative center for what was known as Belad Ghamd, with the seat of local government transferring from El-Zafir to Baljurashi in 1925 under Saudi administration. The research also includes unrelated biographical information about historical figures named Baha' al-Din from different periods and regions, but these do not constitute a family lineage connected to the Gulf region or the Al Baha designation as requested.
data.description: No verifiable information about an Al Baha family exists in the provided research materials.

data.history: The Tunaij (الطنيج) is an Arab tribe that settled primarily in the inland oasis town of Dhaid in Sharjah, as well as in the Ras Al Khaimah town of Rams, with a smaller presence in Hamriyah. At the turn of the 19th century, there were approximately 4,000 Tunaij in the Northern Emirates, of whom 1,500 were Bedouin. The tribe was influential in tribal politics due to their ability to raise a force of some 500 fighting men. The Bedouin Tunaij used Dhaid as their center, where they maintained around 70 houses protected by a fortified tower, sharing the town with the Na'im tribe who had a similar arrangement. At the turn of the 20th century, Dhaid consisted of some 140 houses owned by sections of the Tunaij, Bani Qitab and Khawatir tribes. The Tunaij of Rams were primarily engaged in pearl fishing, and during pearling seasons, both Bedouin Tunaij and Shihuh would come to the coast to work as seasonal labor.
data.description: The Tunaij is an Arab tribe of the UAE that primarily settled in Dhaid, Sharjah, and Rams in Ras Al Khaimah. Historically numbering around 4,000 members at the turn of the 19th century, they were influential in tribal politics and engaged in both Bedouin lifestyle and pearl fishing.
data.modernStatus: In contemporary times, the Al Tunaiji family maintains administrative positions, with Ali Musabah Al Tunaiji serving as director of Al Dhaid Municipality, responsible for implementing residential regulations in the city.

data.history: Based on the available research, the Al Zarouni family appears to have established a notable presence in the UAE's institutional and charitable sectors. The most prominent documented member is Mohammad Abdullah Al Haj Al Zarouni, who serves as Head of Emirates Red Crescent and was among the founding members of the Sunni Cultural Center (Markaz Dubai) when it opened on April 22, 2010. This religious and cultural center operates under the supervision of Dubai's Islamic Affairs and Charitable Activities Department, providing educational services, family counseling, and hosting charitable events for the Muslim community in Dubai.

data.history: The Choithram family emerged as a prominent business dynasty through the entrepreneurial efforts of Thakurdas Choithram Pagarani, born in India in 1914. A devout Hindu, Pagarani left India around 1937 and established a highly successful supermarket business in Sierra Leone, eventually expanding the enterprises worldwide under the name 'T. Choithram and Sons' or simply 'Choithrams'. By 1989, he had consolidated most of his business operations under offshore holding companies, including T Choithram International SA, while maintaining majority shareholding. The family's business empire became internationally recognized, with operations spanning multiple continents and generating substantial wealth that Pagarani intended to use for philanthropic purposes through the establishment of the Choithram International Foundation before his death in 1992.
data.description: The Choithram family is an Indian-origin business dynasty that built a global commercial empire through supermarket and trading operations, founded by Thakurdas Choithram Pagarani who left India in 1937 to establish successful businesses in Sierra Leone and internationally.
data.originStory: Thakurdas Choithram Pagarani was born in India in 1914 and left around 1937 to establish business operations in Sierra Leone, where he built the foundation of what would become a global commercial empire.
data.modernStatus: The family operates through T. Choithram International SA and associated companies, with T. Choithram Hospital in Indore providing medical services and supporting charitable activities including pediatric heart surgery programs.

data.history: The Al Shehhi family originates from the Shihuh tribe, a tribal group with documented presence in the northern Emirates region. The research provides limited historical information about the family's broader development, focusing primarily on one tragic figure from the late 20th century. Marwan al-Shehhi was born in 1978 in Al Qusaidat, Ras Al Khaimah, United Arab Emirates, as the son of an Arab imam father and an Egyptian mother. He was described as being part of the Shihuh tribe through his father's side, though specific details about the family's historical role in the region remain sparse in available records. The family appears to have maintained traditional religious and cultural connections, with Marwan's father serving as an imam in the local community.

data.history: The Hussain family represents multiple distinct lineages across the Gulf and broader Islamic world, with no single unified origin or migration pattern. The name derives from the Arabic root ḥ-s-n meaning 'good' or 'handsome', and was first used by Prophet Muhammad for his grandson Husayn ibn Ali. In the UAE, Hussain Sajwani represents a prominent business lineage - born in 1952-1953 as the eldest of five children to entrepreneurial parents, with his father operating as a trader in the local souk. Sajwani obtained education in Baghdad and the University of Washington before establishing himself in the UAE's emerging real estate sector. In Kuwait, the Al-Musallam branch produces significant sports administrators, while other Hussain families have established themselves across various Gulf states in different capacities. The family name appears across multiple unrelated lineages throughout the region, each with distinct origins and migration patterns that cannot be consolidated into a single narrative.
data.description: Multiple distinct Hussain family lineages across the Gulf region, primarily known through UAE business magnate Hussain Sajwani and Kuwaiti sports administrator Husain Al-Musallam, with no unified tribal or geographic origin.
data.modernStatus: Prominent in UAE real estate development through DAMAC Properties (founded 2002), with Hussain Sajwani holding $10.2 billion net worth as of 2025. Active in Gulf sports administration and various business sectors across different family branches.
data.nameEtymology: Derives from Arabic root ḥ-s-n (ح س ن) meaning 'good', 'handsome' or 'beautiful', being the diminutive of Hassan. First used by Prophet Muhammad for his grandson Husayn ibn Ali after being commanded by Allah through archangel Gabriel.

data.history: The Al Rushaid family is a prominent Kuwaiti business and political family with significant influence in Kuwait's financial and economic sectors. Abdulwahab Al-Rushaid, born October 29, 1984, represents the modern political generation of the family, serving as Kuwait's Minister of Finance and Minister of State for Economics Affairs and Investments from December 28, 2021 to April 2023. During his tenure, he headed the Kuwait Investment Authority, which manages Kuwait's $700 billion sovereign wealth fund designed to reduce dependence on oil-related investments. The family has established significant business interests internationally, with Al Rushaid Group providing financing for major infrastructure companies like Cleveland Bridge & Engineering Company, where they held an 88.5% stake by September 2002, and forming joint ventures with companies like International SOS in 2018 to provide medical services in Saudi Arabia. Historical diplomatic connections include Yacoub Abdulaziz al-Rushaid, who served as the first Kuwaiti Ambassador to India in June 1962, establishing early diplomatic relations between Kuwait and India after Kuwaiti independence.
data.description: The Al Rushaid family is an influential Kuwaiti business and political dynasty involved in finance, international investments, and diplomatic relations, with significant holdings in construction and infrastructure companies globally.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains significant political influence with Abdulwahab Al-Rushaid serving as a key economic minister and board member of Kuwait Economic Society. They operate Al Rushaid Group, which has major international investments including infrastructure companies and joint ventures in Saudi Arabia and other Gulf states.

data.history: The Al Qatami family has produced several notable figures in Kuwait's modern history, particularly in education, politics, and the arts. The most prominent member is Lulwah Al-Qatami (born 1933/34), who became the first woman from Kuwait to attend university abroad when she studied at the University of Edinburgh in the 1950s. She played a crucial role in Kuwait's women's rights movement, leading protests against mandatory abaya wearing for female teachers, including the public burning of abayas in 1956. Al-Qatami founded the Kuwait Women's Cultural & Social Society in 1963 and served as the first Director of the College of Women at Kuwait University from 1975 to 1993. The family has also been involved in politics, with Jassim Al-Qatami serving as a member of parliament who participated in interrogating government ministers in 1986. In the contemporary period, the family has connections to the arts through figures like Aziz Al Qatami, who was a founding member of the GCC art collective in 2013. The Al-Qatami name appears in various contexts of Kuwaiti civil society and political development throughout the 20th and 21st centuries.
data.description: A Kuwaiti family prominent in education, women's rights activism, and politics, best known for producing Kuwait's first female university graduate abroad and founding members of key women's organizations.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains prominence in Kuwaiti civil society through educational leadership, women's rights advocacy, and cultural organizations, with continued involvement in academia and the arts.

data.history: The Al Sarraf family name derives from the Arabic word 'al-sarraf' (الصرّاف), meaning 'money changer,' which was the profession of Zaki al-Sarraf's father, Abd al-Husayn al-Sarraf, who owned a bureau de change. Zaki Abd al-Husayn Bedget al-Asadi (1932-1996), better known as Zaki al-Sarraf, was a prominent Iraqi journalist and poet who hailed from the Bedget family of the Asad tribe. His ancestors had settled in Karbala more than a thousand years ago, before the battle of Karbala, where they served in the Imam Husayn and al-Abbas shrines and held custodianship of the two holy sites. The family's literary lineage included his great ancestor Mehdi al-Asadi (who became known as Haaj Mehdi Bedget after a speech error), his great uncle Jawad Bedget (1795-1864) who was a prominent poet, and his uncle Mashkoor al-Asadi, also a renowned journalist. The documented family lineage traces: Zakī bin ʿAbd al-Ḥusayn bin Mehdi bin Ḥammoud bin Muḥammad-Ḥusayn bin ʿAbd al-Nabī bin Mehdī (Bedget) bin Sāleḥ bin ʿAlī bin [leading to] Asad bin Khuzayma bin Mudrika bin Ilyās bin Mudhar bin Nizār bin Maʿad bin ʿAdnān. The research also identifies other individuals with the Al Sarraf surname, including Palestinian-Canadian DJ Ossama al-Sarraf and Lebanese politician Yacoub Sarraf, though their specific family connections to the Iraqi branch are not established in the available sources.
data.description: Al Sarraf is a family name meaning 'money changer' in Arabic, with documented branches including an Iraqi literary family from the Asad tribe based in Karbala, and other individuals across the Middle East and diaspora communities.
data.originStory: The Al Sarraf ancestors settled in Karbala more than a thousand years ago, before the battle of Karbala, where they served in the Imam Husayn and al-Abbas shrines and held custodianship of the two holy sites. The family belongs to the prominent literary Bedget family of the Asad tribe.
data.modernStatus: The family includes notable figures in journalism, literature, music, and politics across various countries, with documented members in Iraq, Palestine, Lebanon, and diaspora communities in Canada.

folkLegends.title: The Origin of the Bedget Name
folkLegends.story: The family's great ancestor, Mehdi al-Asadi, was a renowned poet who became known as Haaj Mehdi Bedget after making a speech error during a poetry performance. Instead of saying 'bezeghet' (Arabic: بزغت), he said 'bedeqet' (Arabic: بدقت), and because of that incident, he became known as Haaj Mehdi Bedget.

data.history: The Dashti family represents a prominent Iranian-origin family with significant presence in both Iran and Kuwait. The family's roots trace back to the Dashti region in Bushehr province, Iran, as evidenced by notable figures like Ali Dashti (1897-1982), born in Dashti, Bushehr, who became a prominent Iranian writer, politician, and senator during the Pahlavi dynasty. The family's migration to Kuwait appears to have occurred as part of the broader 'Ajam (Persian) migration patterns to the Gulf states, particularly during the 19th and early 20th centuries when many Iranian families established themselves as merchants and professionals in Kuwait. In Kuwait, the Dashti family has achieved considerable prominence in politics, economics, and academia. The most notable Kuwaiti family member is Rola Dashti (born 1964), whose father Abdullah Ali Dashti is described as 'Kuwaiti of Iranian origins' with a Lebanese mother, making her part of the Iranian-origin community in Kuwait. She became one of the first female MPs elected to the Kuwaiti parliament in 2009 and later served as minister of state planning and development affairs. The family's presence extends beyond these two countries, with members like Gohar Dashti (born 1980) representing the artistic and cultural contributions of the Dashti lineage in contemporary Iran. The family exemplifies the transnational character of many Gulf families, maintaining connections to their Iranian heritage while establishing significant roles in their adopted countries' political, economic, and cultural spheres.
data.description: An Iranian-origin family with prominent members in both Iran and Kuwait, known for contributions to politics, literature, economics, and the arts across multiple generations.
data.originStory: The Dashti family originates from the Dashti region in Bushehr province, Iran, as confirmed by Ali Dashti's birth in 1897 in this area. The family's migration to Kuwait occurred as part of the broader 'Ajam Persian community settlement in the Gulf states.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains prominent positions in politics and academia, with Rola Dashti serving as a former Kuwaiti minister and MP, while other members like Abdul-Hamid Dashti have served in the Kuwait National Assembly. The family is also represented in arts and sports internationally.
data.nameEtymology: The surname derives from the Dashti region in Bushehr province, Iran, indicating geographical origins.

data.history: The Khajeh-Nouri family is one of the most prominent aristocratic families in contemporary Iranian history, emerging from the ancient imperial era and originating from the Baladeh area in Mazandaran. The family held local power during the late Safavid, Afshari, and Zand dynasties, with the oldest known ancestor being Haji Mohammad Akbar during the late Safavid era. His son Khajeh Abdal Bey served as 'ruler of Aghasi' during the reign of Shah Abbas II and was ruler of Behshahr. From the beginning of the Qajar era, the family entered politics through bureaucratic and military positions, becoming based in Tehran where they served in the Qajar court. The most famous figure was Mirza Aga Khan Nouri, who served as Prime Minister of Iran during the reign of Naser al-Din Shah Qajar. Throughout the Qajar and Pahlavi eras, the Khajeh Nouri family held high political positions and maintained strong relations with the ruling dynasties, belonging to the elite group called the 'Thousand Families' by opponents of the monarchy before the Islamic Revolution. During and after the 1979 revolution, when many family members were executed by the regime, survivors fled permanently to Europe, London, and the United States, where the family already held assets and had been educated.
data.description: The Khajeh-Nouri family is a prominent aristocratic Iranian family from Mazandaran that held high political positions from the Safavid through Pahlavi eras, with members serving as prime ministers and court officials. Many fled Iran after the 1979 revolution.
data.originStory: The family originated from the Baladeh area in Mazandaran during the ancient imperial era, with documented history dating back to the late Safavid period under Shah Abbas II when their ancestor Khajeh Abdal Bey served as ruler of Aghasi and Behshahr.
data.modernStatus: After the Islamic Revolution, surviving family members permanently settled outside Iran in Europe, London, and the United States where they had existing assets and educational ties. Many were executed during the revolution.
data.nameEtymology: The name translates to 'Lord of Light' in Persian

migrationPath.description: Family members fled Iran during and after the Islamic Revolution to countries where they already held assets

data.history: According to oral tradition from Al Khor, the Al Baddah family is descended from the Al Muhannadi tribe, one of the founding tribes of Al Khor (originally known as Khor Al Shaqiq) in mid-18th century Qatar. The settlement was established around 1750 when two Al Muhannadi tribesmen, Mohammed bin Baddah and Majid Al Shuqairi, discovered fresh water at Ain Hleetan while searching for missing camels. In the 19th century, as other tribes migrated to Khor Al Shaqiq due to its reputation and well-known spring, several tribes including Shahwan and Bani Hajer intermarried with the Al Muhannadi and formed new families, including the Al-Baddha (Al Baddah). Around 1850, many of these tribes united to form the Al Muhannadi tribal confederation. The family appears to have maintained its connection to Al Khor throughout the 20th century, with the area serving as a stronghold for various political movements and continuing to be dominated by Al Muhannadi descendants.
data.description: The Al Baddah family traces its origins to the Al Muhannadi tribe and the founding of Al Khor, Qatar in the mid-18th century. They are part of a tribal confederation that formed through intermarriage with other tribes in the 19th century.
data.originStory: According to Al Khor oral tradition, the family name derives from Mohammed bin Baddah, one of two Al Muhannadi tribesmen who discovered the fresh water source at Ain Hleetan around 1750, leading to the founding of what became Al Khor. The story states they were searching for missing camels when they found the water source and suitable docking platform that led to the entire Al Muhannadi tribe relocating there.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains its historical connection to Al Khor, which has evolved from a pearling and fishing village into a major Qatari city serving the oil and gas industry, with Al Khor now being the capital of Al Khor and Al Thakhira municipality.

folkLegends.title: The Founding of Al Khor
folkLegends.story: Two Al Muhannadi tribesmen, Mohammed bin Baddah and Majid Al Shuqairi, discovered Al Khor while searching for missing camels around 1750. They found fresh water at Ain Hleetan and a suitable docking platform, leading to the entire tribe relocating there. An alternative version states they discovered the area while chasing a hare rather than searching for camels.

data.nameEtymology: The family name Al Baddah derives from Mohammed bin Baddah, one of the founding figures of Al Khor according to oral tradition.

migrationPath.description: Al Muhannadi tribe relocated from Al Khuwayr to establish Al Khor after discovering fresh water source

data.history:
The Al Qenaei family appears to have been established in Kuwait by at least the early 20th century, with evidence of their presence during the crucial political developments of 1938-1939. The family was involved in Kuwait's early democratic movements, participating in the historic Legislative Council elections of 1938. During the tumultuous period surrounding Kuwait's first attempts at parliamentary governance, family members were present during the political upheaval that led to the dissolution of the elected councils and the subsequent crackdown by Sheikh Ahmad Al-Jaber Al-Sabah in March 1939.

In the modern era, the Al Qenaei family has produced notable figures in Kuwait's sporting history. Abdulrahman Al-Dawla Al-Mutawa Al-Qenaei emerged as a prominent footballer in the 1960s, becoming the first player to score an official goal for the Kuwait national football team. His athletic achievements helped establish the family's recognition in contemporary Kuwaiti society, representing a transition from their earlier political involvement to contributions in sports and cultural life.

The family name suggests a compound identity with the Al-Mutawa designation, indicating possible religious or scholarly heritage alongside their Al Qenaei tribal identity. Their sustained presence in Kuwait from the pre-oil era through the modern period demonstrates their integration into Kuwaiti society and their adaptation to the country's rapid transformation in the 20th century.
data.description: The Al Qenaei family is a Kuwaiti family with documented presence from the 1930s political movements through modern times. They are known for their involvement in Kuwait's early democratic experiments and later contributions to national sports.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains presence in Kuwait with documented contributions to sports and athletics, particularly through Abdulrahman Al-Dawla's pioneering role in Kuwaiti football history.

timelineEvents.description: Family members participated in Kuwait's first democratic elections during the Majlis movement

timelineEvents.description: Family witnessed the violent end of Kuwait's first democratic experiment

data.history: The Al Sabti family appears to be connected to Mandaean religious traditions based on the Wikipedia entry for Rafid al-Sabti, an Iraqi-Dutch Mandaean priest. Rafid al-Sabti was born in 1965 in Baghdad, Iraq, to Rishama Abdullah, son of Sheikh Neǧm. He was initiated into the Mandaean priesthood by his father and later emigrated to the Netherlands, where he serves as a priest in Nijmegen. The family maintains important religious manuscripts and continues Mandaean scholarly traditions, with Rafid serving as custodian of the Rbai Rafid Collection of Mandaean manuscripts. His son Ardwan Al-Sabti has become a researcher and graphic designer known for creating Mandaic fonts, indicating the family's continued involvement in preserving Mandaean culture and language.
data.description: The Al Sabti family represents a Mandaean priestly lineage from Iraq, with documented religious leadership roles and scholarly contributions to Mandaean literature and manuscript preservation.
data.originStory: Based on available research, the Al Sabti family has roots in the Mandaean religious community of Iraq, specifically Baghdad, with a documented priestly lineage including Sheikh Neǧm and his son Rishama Abdullah.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains religious and scholarly roles within the Mandaean community, with members serving as priests, researchers, and cultural preservationists in both Iraq and the Netherlands.

data.history: The research provides limited information about the Al Barjas family specifically. The available sources mention two notable individuals with the Barjas name from different contexts. Sheikh Barjas Shaher Sayel Al-Hadid (born 1936) is a Jordanian politician and Sheikh of the Al-Hadid tribe, who served in various military and political roles including the Jordanian Parliament. His family history is intertwined with Jordanian history, as his father Shaher and cousin Minwer fought in the Arab Revolt that installed King Abdullah as ruler, establishing a close relationship with the Hashemite royal family. Additionally, there is mention of Abd al-Karim Barjas al-Rawi, who served as the first post-invasion Governor of Iraq's Al Anbar province until August 2004. The connection between these individuals and any broader Al Barjas family lineage in the Gulf region is not established in the available research.
data.description: Based on available research, Al Barjas appears as a surname associated with notable political figures in Jordan and Iraq, though comprehensive family genealogy and Gulf connections are not documented in the sources.
data.modernStatus: Limited information available - individuals with Barjas surname have held significant political positions in Jordan and Iraq
