data.history: The Al Shehab family mentioned in the research appears to be connected to the Syrian television series 'Bab Al-Hara,' where Abu Shehab is portrayed as a character who becomes the leader in Hara after the death of the previous leader, marries Sharifa in season 3, and dies in season 4 after accidentally being killed by Abu Draa. The character is played by actor Samer al-Masry and appears in seasons 1-3 of the series. However, this appears to be a fictional portrayal in a Syrian drama series set in Damascus during the inter-war period under French rule. The research also references the historical Shihab dynasty, an Arab family that served as Emirs of Mount Lebanon from the early 18th to mid-19th century during Ottoman rule, but no direct connection is established between this historical dynasty and any Al Shehab family in the GCC region.
data.description: Al Shehab appears in the research primarily as a fictional character in the Syrian television series 'Bab Al-Hara,' with no verified information about an actual GCC family of this name.

data.history: The Boushahri family appears to be established in Kuwait's cultural and artistic landscape, with documented involvement in the contemporary art scene. Abdullah Boushahri is listed among notable Kuwaiti film directors, indicating the family's participation in Kuwait's cinema industry. The family operates the Boushahri Gallery, which is recognized as one of Kuwait's established art galleries in the country's vibrant contemporary art scene. Kuwait has experienced significant cultural development since the 1930s, when the modern art movement emerged, making it the oldest such movement in the Arabian Peninsula. The timing of the Boushahri family's involvement in these cultural sectors aligns with Kuwait's broader cultural renaissance and the establishment of its entertainment and arts infrastructure.
data.description: The Boushahri family is involved in Kuwait's cultural sector, with notable presence in cinema and contemporary art through gallery operations.
data.modernStatus: The family operates the Boushahri Gallery in Kuwait's art scene and has representation in the film industry through Abdullah Boushahri, who is listed as a Kuwaiti director.

data.history: Based on the provided research, Al Abdulla refers to one of the two main branches of the Al Khalifa ruling family of Bahrain, descended from Abdulla bin Ahmed Al Khalifa. Following the death of Ahmed bin Muhammad bin Khalifa in 1796, his sons Salman and Abdulla moved to Bahrain and co-ruled it as feudal estates, with Abdulla settling in Muharraq Island while Salman settled in Bahrain Island. The Al Khalifa soon became split into two branches - Al-Abdulla and Al-Salman - that engaged in open conflict between 1842 and 1846. The Al-Salman branch emerged victorious and enjoyed complete rule of Bahrain, while the Al-Abdulla branch continued as rivals until 1895. According to the research, Abdulla ibn Ahmad Al Khalifa's descendants now live in Qatar, having been displaced from their original seat of power in Bahrain. The Al Khalifa family originated from the Anizah tribe and were part of the Utub alliance that migrated from Najd in central Arabia to Kuwait, then ruled Al Zubarah in Qatar before settling in Bahrain following their conquest of the islands in 1783.
data.description: Al Abdulla represents one of the two main branches of Bahrain's ruling Al Khalifa family, descended from Abdulla bin Ahmed Al Khalifa who co-ruled Bahrain in the late 18th century. After losing a succession conflict in the mid-19th century, this branch's descendants now primarily reside in Qatar.
data.originStory: The Al Abdulla branch originates from Abdulla bin Ahmed Al Khalifa, son of Ahmed bin Muhammad bin Khalifa who first conquered Bahrain in 1783. Following Ahmed's death in 1796, Abdulla and his brother Salman moved to Bahrain and established co-rule, with Abdulla settling in Muharraq Island and ruling it independently until the family split led to conflict and their eventual displacement.
data.modernStatus: The Al Abdulla branch descendants now primarily live in Qatar, having been displaced from political power in Bahrain following their defeat by the Al-Salman branch in the mid-19th century succession conflicts.
data.nameEtymology: Al Abdulla means 'family/descendants of Abdulla,' referring to Abdulla bin Ahmed Al Khalifa, son of the founder of Al Khalifa rule in Bahrain.

data.history: The Al Musallam family has significant representation in both Kuwait and Bahrain, with documented political and administrative roles spanning multiple generations. In Kuwait, Husain Al-Musallam (born 1960) represents a prominent branch of the family, serving in high-level sports administration including Director General of the Olympic Council of Asia and president of World Aquatics. His career began as an airline pilot with Kuwait Airways in 1978, progressing to captain by 1988, while simultaneously building his sports administration credentials from 1982. In Bahrain, Ahmed bin Salman Al-Musallam (born 1961) represents another significant branch, serving as Speaker of the Council of Representatives since December 2022. He comes from a politically connected family - his father Salman bin Jabr Al-Musallam was a high-ranking Ministry of Interior officer, and his brother Khalid served as Bahraini ambassador to Morocco. The Bahraini branch has deep roots in security and public service, with Ahmed having attended King Fahd Security College in Riyadh and serving as a Ministry of Interior officer before entering business and politics. While both branches share the Al Musallam surname and prominence in Gulf politics, the research does not establish a direct genealogical connection between the Kuwaiti and Bahraini branches. Historical context suggests the Al Musallam were noted as a dominant tribe in northwestern Qatar, mentioned in connection with the Bani Utbah confederation's early settlements, though specific lineage connections to the modern political families are not documented in the available research.
data.description: The Al Musallam family has prominent branches in Kuwait and Bahrain, with notable figures in sports administration, politics, and government service spanning multiple generations in Gulf leadership roles.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains high-profile positions in Gulf politics and international organizations. In Kuwait, they hold leadership in Olympic sports administration and aviation. In Bahrain, they occupy senior parliamentary positions, with strong backgrounds in security services and business.

data.history: The Al Mudaifer family appears to have established roots in Buraydah, the capital of Al-Qassim Province in north-central Saudi Arabia. The most prominent contemporary member is Abdullah Al Mudaifer, born in 1982 in Buraydah, who became a well-known television presenter, actor, columnist and journalist. He holds a bachelor's degree in Sharia and Law but chose a career in media over legal practice. In November 2024, another family member, Aimen Al-Mudaifer, was appointed as the new CEO of the NEOM megaproject, replacing Nadhmi Al-Nasr who had been removed from his position as head of the ambitious Saudi Vision 2030 development. Aimen Al-Mudaifer previously served as head of the Local Real Estate division at Saudi Arabia's Public Investment Fund before taking on the leadership of the troubled NEOM project, which had faced extensive problems including cost overruns, delays, and management issues.
data.description: The Al Mudaifer family is based in Buraydah, Saudi Arabia, with notable members in media and government development projects. Abdullah Al Mudaifer is a prominent television presenter, while Aimen Al-Mudaifer leads the NEOM megaproject.
data.modernStatus: The family has achieved prominence in Saudi media and government development projects. Abdullah Al Mudaifer is a celebrated television host known for programs like Al Liwan and Al-Soura, and conducted a high-profile interview with Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman. Aimen Al-Mudaifer holds a senior executive position, appointed in November 2024 as CEO of the NEOM megaproject under Saudi Vision 2030.

data.history: The Salahuddin family is a prominent Maldivian family descended from Hussain Salahuddin (1881-1948), an influential writer, poet, essayist and scholar who became one of the most prolific writers of early modern Maldivian literature during the 'Era of Crawling'. Hussain Salahuddin obtained his education on the island of Meedhoo and became Attorney General of the Maldives at age 18, later serving as Chief Justice, Secretary and Prime Minister for the Majlis. He founded and served as principal of Majeediyya School in Malé, the first institution of formal education in the Maldives, which was started on the front veranda of his residence Bageechaage. The family became deeply embedded in Maldivian politics and literature, with Hussain Salahuddin's children from his three wives becoming prominent writers, poets and government officials. His daughters Mariyam Saeed and Fathimath Saeed both served as First Ladies of the Maldives, married to presidents Ibrahim Nasir and Mohamed Amin Didi respectively. His son Ibrahim Shihab served as vice president from 1975-1977, while other children held positions as parliamentarians and ministers. The family's influence continued into subsequent generations, with grandchildren serving as Chief Justice, parliamentarians, and other high government positions, establishing the Salahuddin name as one of the most politically influential families in modern Maldivian history.
data.description: The Salahuddin family is a prominent Maldivian political and literary dynasty descended from scholar Hussain Salahuddin (1881-1948). The family has produced two First Ladies, a vice president, multiple ministers, and continues to hold influential positions in Maldivian politics and judiciary.
data.originStory: The family originates from Hussain Salahuddin, born on 14 April 1881, who signed his writings as Salahuddin Hussein bin Moosa al-Mahli, meaning 'Salahuddin Hussain, son of Moosa from Malé'. He obtained his education on the island of Meedhoo under Al-Allam al-Shaikh al-Hafiz Ibrahim Thakurufaan and later studied under al-Shaikh Muhamed Jamaluddin Naib Thutthu.
data.modernStatus: The family remains highly influential in Maldivian politics, with recent generations serving as Chief Justice (Ahmed Muthasim Adnan served from 2019-2025), parliamentarians, and other senior government positions. The family maintains strong connections to both political leadership and the legal/educational establishment.
data.nameEtymology: The name Salahuddin appears to be adopted from the Arabic name meaning 'righteousness of faith', as evidenced by Hussain Salahuddin's formal signature as Salahuddin Hussein bin Moosa al-Mahli in Arabic.

data.history: The research provides information about several distinct Ahmed families/individuals, but no single coherent Ahmed family lineage in the GCC context. The most prominent GCC-related Ahmed figure is Ahmed bin Muhammad bin Khalifa (Ahmed al-Fateh), born in Kuwait in the first half of the 18th century, who became the founder of Bahrain's ruling Al Khalifa family. He conquered Bahrain in 1783 after defeating Nasr Al-Madhkur, who had besieged Zubarah. Ahmed ruled from Al Zubarah on the Qatari Peninsula, spending winters there and summers in Bahrain until his death in 1795. His descendants split into two main branches - Al-Abdulla and Al-Salman - with the Al-Salman branch eventually gaining complete control of Bahrain. The research also mentions other Ahmed figures including Iraqi-Kurdish politician Shanaz Ibrahim Ahmed (born 1954), whose father Ibrahim Ahmed was a prominent Kurdish judge and politician, and Egyptian-American comedian Ahmed Ahmed, but these appear unrelated to any GCC family structure.
data.description: Multiple distinct Ahmed individuals and families identified in research, with the most significant being Ahmed al-Fateh, founder of Bahrain's ruling Al Khalifa dynasty in 1783.
data.originStory: Ahmed bin Muhammad bin Khalifa was born in Kuwait in the first half of the 18th century. He was based in Al Zubarah on the Qatari Peninsula, which his father had built after departing Kuwait, before conquering Bahrain in 1783.
data.tribalOrigin: Anizah tribe (for Al Khalifa branch only)
data.modernStatus: The Al Khalifa descendants of Ahmed al-Fateh currently rule the Kingdom of Bahrain, with King Hamad bin Isa Al Khalifa as head of state since 1999.
data.nameEtymology: Ahmed al-Fateh means 'Ahmed the Conqueror' for his conquest of Bahrain

data.history: The Ebrahim family has diverse historical manifestations across the Gulf region, with notable branches in Iran, Bahrain, and other areas. The most documented lineage is that of Hajji Ebrahim Shirazi (1745-1801), whose family originated from Qazvin but migrated to Shiraz in the mid-13th century due to persecution of Jews. His ancestor married the daughter of Qavam al-Din Hassan Shirazi, minister of the Muzaffarid dynasty, through which the family inherited estates in Shiraz and gradually became kadkhodas (wardens). Ebrahim's grandfather Asher was a merchant who converted to Islam and renamed himself Mahmoud, while his father Mohammad Hashem served as kadkhoda of the Balakaft quarter in Shiraz. Hajji Ebrahim himself became a pivotal figure in Iranian politics, serving as kalantar of Shiraz and later as the first grand vizier of Qajar Iran, earning the title E'temad ol-Dowleh. After his execution in 1801 along with most of his family, his surviving son Ali Akbar was granted the title Qavam ol-Molk, and his descendants formed the influential Qavam family. In Bahrain, there exists a separate Jewish Ebrahim lineage, exemplified by the family of Houda Ezra Ebrahim Nonoo, whose grandfather Ibrahim left Baghdad in 1888 to establish a financial business in Bahrain, representing one of the small Jewish communities that settled in the Gulf region.
data.description: The Ebrahim family represents multiple distinct lineages across the Gulf region, including the prominent Shirazi political dynasty in Iran descended from Jewish converts to Islam, and Jewish merchant families in Bahrain with Iraqi origins.
data.originStory: The Shirazi Ebrahim family originated from Qazvin but migrated to Shiraz in the mid-13th century when one ancestor, fleeing persecution of Jews, married the daughter of Qavam al-Din Hassan Shirazi, minister of the Muzaffarid dynasty. Through this marriage, they inherited estates and gradually became kadkhodas (wardens). The Bahraini Ebrahim branch traces to Ibrahim who left Baghdad in 1888 to establish business in Bahrain.
data.modernStatus: The Shirazi branch evolved into the influential Qavam family in Iran after 1801, while Bahraini branches include successful business families and political figures, with Houda Nonoo serving as Bahrain's ambassador to the United States from 2008-2013, becoming the first Jewish ambassador of any Middle Eastern Arab country.
data.nameEtymology: Ebrahim is the Arabic/Persian form of Abraham, reflecting the family's historical Jewish origins before conversion to Islam in some branches.

migrationPath.description: Migration due to persecution of Jews, leading to marriage with Muzaffarid minister's daughter
Ibrahim Nonoo established financial business in Bahrain

data.history:
The Saeed family refers to Sheikh Saeed bin Maktoum bin Hasher Al Maktoum (1878-1958), who served as the longest-ruling Ruler of Dubai from 1912 until his death in 1958. He was the son of Maktoum bin Hasher, who ruled Dubai from 1894 to 1906, establishing the family's direct lineage within the Al Maktoum ruling dynasty. Saeed's accession to power came through family succession when his cousin Butti bin Suhail Al Maktoum died in 1912, with the transition managed by Sheikh Butti bin Rashid Al Maktoum.

Sheikh Saeed presided over Dubai during a critical period of economic transformation, navigating the collapse of the traditional pearling industry and successfully transitioning Dubai into an active trading hub. His rule faced significant challenges including a plague outbreak in 1913 that killed approximately 1,300 people, the gradual decline of the pearling trade from 1910 onwards, and internal political pressure culminating in the Majlis movement of 1938-1939. Despite these challenges, he maintained power and oversaw Dubai's emergence as a prosperous trading center.

In his later years, Sheikh Saeed increasingly delegated administrative responsibilities to his son Rashid bin Saeed Al Maktoum, who became the de facto ruler following World War II and would eventually succeed him as ruler in 1958. The family established important matrimonial alliances, including Rashid's marriage to Sheikha Latifa bint Hamdan Al Nahyan from Abu Dhabi's ruling family, strengthening inter-emirate relationships that would prove crucial during the formation of the United Arab Emirates.
data.description: The Al Maktoum ruling family of Dubai, specifically the lineage of Sheikh Saeed bin Maktoum (1878-1958), who transformed Dubai from a pearling center into a modern trading hub during his 46-year reign.
data.originStory: Sheikh Saeed bin Maktoum bin Hasher Al Maktoum was born into the established Al Maktoum ruling dynasty of Dubai in 1878, inheriting rulership through direct patrilineal succession from his father Maktoum bin Hasher who ruled from 1894-1906.
data.modernStatus: The Saeed lineage continues through the current ruling family of Dubai, with Sheikh Saeed's descendants including Sheikh Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum (current ruler) and other prominent figures in UAE governance and business.

data.history: The Sámi are the traditionally Sámi-speaking indigenous people inhabiting the region of Sápmi, which encompasses large northern parts of Norway, Sweden, Finland, and the Kola Peninsula in Russia. The western Uralic languages are believed to have spread from the original Proto-Uralic homeland along the Volga in the second and third quarters of the 2nd millennium BC, with groups moving northwest using ancient river routes. The Sámi language first developed on the southern side of Lake Onega and Lake Ladoga and spread from there, with the Sámi people arriving in their current homeland during the Bronze Age or early Iron Age. The first probable historical mention was by Tacitus around AD 98, naming them Fenni. For centuries, the Sámi maintained relatively separate lives from Scandinavians, with the Sámi primarily living in the inland northern regions while Scandinavians gradually colonized the Norwegian coast. From the 19th century, Norwegian and Swedish authorities implemented aggressive Scandinavization policies aimed at forced assimilation, banning Sámi language and culture in many contexts. The 1979 Alta controversy brought Sámi rights onto the political agenda, leading to the creation of Sámi parliaments and greater recognition of indigenous rights.
data.description: The Sámi are the indigenous people of northern Scandinavia and the Kola Peninsula, traditionally engaged in reindeer herding, fishing, and hunting. They speak Sámi languages of the Uralic family and have faced centuries of forced assimilation policies.
data.originStory: The Sámi trace their origins to western Uralic-speaking peoples who migrated northwest from the Proto-Uralic homeland along the Volga River in the 2nd millennium BC. These groups used ancient river routes through northern Russia, with some settling between Karelia, Ladoga and Lake Ilmen. The groups that reached the Finnish Lakeland from 1600-1500 BC later became the Sámi, arriving in their current homeland during the Bronze Age or early Iron Age.
data.modernStatus: The Sámi have established parliaments in Finland (1973), Norway (1989), and Sweden (1993) as representative bodies. About 2,800 people are engaged in reindeer herding in Norway, legally protected as an exclusive Sámi livelihood. The estimated total Sámi population is about 70,000 across the Nordic region, with roughly half living in Norway.
data.nameEtymology: The current consensus among specialists is that the word Sámi was borrowed from the Proto-Baltic word *žēmē, meaning 'land' (cognate with Slavic zemlja). Speakers of Northern Sámi refer to themselves as Sámit (the Sámis) or Sápmelaš (of Sámi kin).

migrationPath.description: Migration of western Uralic peoples from Proto-Uralic homeland using ancient river routes through northern Russia

data.history: The Verma family represents multiple distinct lineages across different regions, with no unified origin or connection between various Verma families mentioned in the research. In the political sphere, the Verma family of Bihar has established significant influence, with Upendra Nath Verma serving as a former Minister in Vishwanath Pratap Singh's cabinet and multiple terms as Member of Bihar Legislative Assembly, including roles as Former Minister For Education in Government of Bihar. His son Bagi Kumar Verma continued the political legacy as Former Minister in Government of Bihar and multiple-term Member of Bihar Legislative Assembly. In Jharkhand, the Verma family has deep political roots through Rati Lal Prasad Verma, who served six terms as Member of Indian Parliament from Kodarma Lok Sabha constituency, with his brother Jagdish Prasad Kushwaha considered a founding leader of Bharatiya Jana Sangh in Jharkhand, and nephew Jai Prakash Verma serving as Former Member of Jharkhand Legislative Assembly from Gandey Assembly constituency. The Rajasthan Verma family traces to Manikya Lal Verma, the First Prime Minister of Undivided Rajasthan and Former MP from Tonk and Chittorgarh, with his wife Naraini Devi Verma serving as Former Member of Parliament and son Deen Bandhu Verma as Former Member of Parliament from Udaipur and Former Member of Legislative Assembly from Kapasan.
data.description: The Verma surname represents multiple unconnected political families across various Indian states, particularly prominent in Bihar, Jharkhand, and Rajasthan, with members serving in various ministerial and parliamentary positions.
data.modernStatus: Multiple Verma families maintain political influence across different Indian states, with current and former members serving in legislative assemblies, parliament, and ministerial positions at state and national levels.

data.history: The Bansal family appears in the research primarily as fictional characters in Indian entertainment media, specifically in the 2025 crime thriller film 'Raat Akeli Hai: The Bansal Murders' and the 2023-2025 television series 'Mann Atisundar'. In these productions, the Bansals are portrayed as wealthy Indian business families involved in media and industrial enterprises. The film depicts the Bansal family as newspaper owners managing 'Prabhat Manthan' and involved in the broader 'Prabhat Group', with family conflicts arising over business assets and succession disputes. The television series presents Divyam Bansal as the son of businessman Jagmohan Bansal, representing a typical wealthy Indian business family narrative common in Indian popular culture. However, one verified real-world figure is Peyush Bansal, born in 1985 into a Hindu Bansal family to parents Bal Kishan and Kiran Bansal. He co-founded Lenskart, an Indian multinational eyewear retail chain based in Gurugram, and became a prominent entrepreneur and investor, appearing as a 'shark' on Shark Tank India and leading Lenskart's IPO in late 2025.
data.description: The Bansal name appears primarily in Indian entertainment media as fictional wealthy business families, with one notable real entrepreneur Peyush Bansal who co-founded the eyewear company Lenskart. No GCC family genealogy or tribal lineage information is available in the research.
data.modernStatus: Peyush Bansal is the co-founder and CEO of Lenskart, based in Gurugram, India, and a prominent investor on Shark Tank India. He led Lenskart's IPO in late 2025, raising over ₹7,200 crores.

data.history: The Al Shukaili family represents a prominent Omani lineage with notable contributions to education, sports, and public service in the Sultanate of Oman. The family has produced distinguished figures across multiple sectors, demonstrating their integration into Oman's modern institutional framework. Dr. Jokha Abdullah Al Shukaili has achieved significant prominence as CEO of the Oman Authority for Academic Accreditation and Quality Assurance of Education, representing the family's involvement in Oman's educational development. The family has also contributed to Oman's sporting achievements, with footballer Sulaiman Said Saif Al-Shukaili representing the national team in multiple AFC competitions including the 2007 and 2011 Asian Cup campaigns.
data.description: Al Shukaili is an Omani family with notable members in education leadership, professional sports, and public service, demonstrating prominence in modern Oman's institutional development.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains prominent positions in Oman's educational sector through Dr. Jokha Abdullah Al Shukaili's leadership role at the national education authority, with additional representation in professional sports through former national footballer Sulaiman Al-Shukaili.

data.history: The research provides limited information about the Al Sadi family. The most prominent member documented is Tariq Khalifa Salim Al-Sadi, born in 2006 in Oman. His father moved the family from Suwayq to Muscat when Tariq was six to pursue his football career. At age nine, Tariq traveled to England for football trials, and his father subsequently sold everything he owned to move the family to England two years later to support his son's career. The family's journey took them through various English football clubs including Birmingham City, Manchester City, Aston Villa, and Wolverhampton Wanderers before Tariq eventually played for various lower-tier English clubs. The research indicates the family made significant sacrifices for Tariq's football ambitions, with his father giving up substantial assets to relocate to England. Beyond this single family's story, no broader historical information about the Al Sadi lineage, tribal origins, or family business activities is provided in the available research.
data.description: The Al Sadi family is documented through footballer Tariq Al-Sadi's story, showing an Omani family from Suwayq who relocated to Muscat and later England to pursue their son's football career.
data.modernStatus: Limited information available; one documented family member pursuing professional football career

data.history: The Al Yahmadi family belongs to the Yahmad clan of the Azd tribe, with documented presence in Oman dating back to the early Islamic period. The family's historical significance is closely tied to the development of Ibadi Islam in Oman, with several notable members holding positions of religious and political authority. During the early Ibadi imamate period (8th-9th centuries), members of the Yahmad clan, including from the Al Yahmadi lineage, served in important governmental and religious roles. The family name appears in various historical contexts related to Omani villages and settlements, particularly in the Ash Sharqiyah region where the village of Al Yahmadi is located near Ibra. In modern times, the Al Yahmadi name continues to be present in Oman, with family members involved in various sectors including sports, as evidenced by footballer Jameel Al-Yahmadi.
data.description: An Omani family of the Yahmad clan within the Azd tribal confederation, historically associated with Ibadi religious leadership and concentrated in the Ash Sharqiyah region of Oman.
data.originStory: The Al Yahmadi family traces its origins to the Yahmad clan of the broader Azd tribal confederation, which has been present in Oman since the early Islamic period and played significant roles in the establishment of Ibadi religious authority in the region.
data.modernStatus: Contemporary family members are found across various sectors in Oman, including sports and local community leadership, maintaining connections to their ancestral regions in Ash Sharqiyah.

folkLegends.title: The Steadfast Falaj
folkLegends.story: The village name Al Yahmadi shares the same origin myth as neighboring Thabti, where the name derives from the Arabic word meaning 'steadfast,' referring to the village's falaj (irrigation channel) that continued flowing during times of drought when other water sources dried up.

data.nameEtymology: The name Al Yahmadi derives from the Yahmad clan designation within the Azd tribal structure, with 'Yahmad' being one of the recognized subdivisions of this major Omani tribal confederation.

data.history: The Al Marhoon family is documented as one of the Al Kathiri tribes historically residing in Dhofar, Oman. According to J.G. Lorimer's 1908 'Gazetteer of the Persian Gulf, Oman and Central Arabia,' the Al Kathiri tribes included Al-Shanfari, Al-Rawas, Al-Marhoon, Bait Fadhil, Al-Mardoof and Al-Hadhri, who lived in both the hills and villages of Dhofar and spoke dialects of Arabic unknown in other parts of the Arabian Peninsula. The family name appears in multiple historical contexts spanning from Oman to Bahrain. In Bahrain's modern political history, an Abdullah Marhoon was killed during the March 1965 Intifada (March Intifada), which was an uprising against British presence sparked by layoffs at the Bahrain Petroleum Company. Additionally, a Majeed Marhoon was documented as a political activist who spent time in Jidda Island prison during the 1960s and 1970s alongside other political figures like Abdulhadi Khalaf. The family name also appears in contemporary contexts, including in Saudi Arabia's Eastern Province sports, where Marhoon Al-Maa is recorded as a handball player for Al-Khaleej FC. The historical presence of the Al Marhoon name across the Gulf region suggests a family with deep roots in the area's political and social fabric.
data.description: Al Marhoon is historically documented as one of the Al Kathiri tribes of Dhofar, Oman, with family members appearing in political movements and cultural contexts across the Gulf region from the 20th century onward.
data.originStory: The Al Marhoon are identified as one of the Al Kathiri tribes that historically inhabited the Dhofar region of Oman, living in both mountainous areas and villages, and speaking distinctive Arabic dialects not found elsewhere in the Arabian Peninsula.
data.modernStatus: Family members are documented in various Gulf countries including Bahrain, Oman, and Saudi Arabia, with involvement in political activities, sports, and other contemporary fields.

data.history: The Al Kharousi family has documented historical connections to Oman's religious and political leadership, particularly through the figure of Imam Salem bin Rashid Al-Kharousi, who was elected imam at Tanuf in May 1913 and led a revolt against Sultan Faisal bin Turki that combined both Hinawi and Ghafiri tribal groups. This revolt occurred during a period when the Sultan had lost control over Oman's interior regions and faced threats from a reconstituted imamate. A manuscript from 1333 AH written by Imam Salem bin Rashid Al-Kharousi has been found at Al-Fiqain Fort in Manah, indicating the family's scholarly and religious activities in the Ad Dakhiliyah region. The family name appears in historical records related to the Ibadi imamate tradition in Oman, suggesting their involvement in the country's religious governance structures. Additionally, al-Khalil ibn Shathan al-Kharusi (r. 1016–1029) was an Ibadi leader who reasserted control over central Oman and drove out the Buyids from coastal regions, demonstrating the family's long-standing political influence in Omani history.
data.description: The Al Kharousi family is historically documented in Oman's religious and political leadership, most notably through Imam Salem bin Rashid Al-Kharousi who led a significant revolt in 1913, and earlier through al-Khalil ibn Shathan al-Kharusi who controlled central Oman in the early 11th century.

data.history: The Al Kiyumi family appears to have historical connections to the Al Hamra region of Oman, based on references to their involvement in water management systems during the early 18th century. According to historical records from Al Hamra, a man from the Al Kiyumi tribe was instrumental in bringing flowing water to the Falaj Al-Hamra irrigation system during the early 13th century Hijri (approximately early 18th century CE). This water source became known as 'Al-Kiyumi's freedom' in recognition of his contribution to the community's water infrastructure. The family name also appears in modern military contexts, with Brigadier Issa bin Saeed Al Kiyumi receiving the Military Order of Achievement (second class) in 2015, indicating continued prominence in Omani military service. However, the available research provides limited information about the family's broader origins, migration patterns, or comprehensive historical development.
data.description: Al Kiyumi is an Omani family with documented historical ties to water management in the Al Hamra region and modern military service, though comprehensive genealogical details are not available in the current research.
data.modernStatus: Contemporary presence includes military service, with at least one family member achieving brigadier rank and receiving military honors in 2015.

data.history: The Al Hadhrami family traces its origins to the Hadhramaut region in southern Yemen, part of the broader Hadhrami ethnographic group indigenous to the Arabian Peninsula. The Hadharem have a long seafaring and trading tradition that predates Islamic times, with extensive migration patterns across the Indian Ocean basin beginning in earnest during the 19th century. Hadhrami communities established themselves throughout the Arabian Peninsula's trading ports, including the Persian Gulf states, where they became prominent in commerce and religious scholarship. The family name 'al-Hadhrami' literally means 'from Hadhramaut,' indicating their geographic origin from this historically significant region that was known for frankincense and myrrh trade routes. In the modern Gulf context, Hadhrami families have maintained their distinct cultural identity while integrating into local societies, often serving as religious scholars, merchants, and community leaders. Their migration to Gulf states was facilitated by traditional trade networks and religious connections, particularly through Islamic scholarly exchanges and Sufi traditions that were strong in both Hadhramaut and the Gulf region.
data.description: Al Hadhrami is a family originating from the Hadhramaut region of Yemen, part of the broader Hadhrami Arab diaspora that spread throughout the Indian Ocean basin and Arabian Peninsula. They are traditionally associated with trade, religious scholarship, and Islamic learning.
data.originStory: The Al Hadhrami family originates from the Hadhramaut region in southern Yemen, home to the Hadhrami people who have inhabited the area since ancient times. Hadhramaut was historically significant as a center of the frankincense trade and Islamic scholarship, with the region converting to Islam during the time of Prophet Muhammad around 630 CE.
data.modernStatus: Hadhrami communities continue to maintain presence in Gulf states as merchants, religious scholars, and community leaders, with some members involved in various business and cultural activities across the region.

folkLegends.title: Origin of Hadhramaut Name
folkLegends.story: The name Hadhramaut is traditionally explained as meaning 'death has come,' derived from a nickname of Amer bin Qahtan, the region's legendary first settler, or alternatively from Biblical Hebrew meaning 'court of death.'

data.nameEtymology: Al-Hadhrami means 'from Hadhramaut,' indicating geographic origin from the Hadhramaut region of Yemen. The name Hadhramaut itself has disputed origins, with folk etymology suggesting it means 'death has come' from Arabic ḥaḍara ('he came') plus mawt ('death'), though scholarly proposals suggest other meanings.

data.history: The Al Badi family name appears in multiple historical and cultural contexts across different regions and time periods, but the research does not establish a unified genealogical connection between these various uses. The name 'Badi' (Arabic: بديع) means 'wonderful' or 'unique' and is one of the 99 Names of God in Islam, which may explain its adoption across different families and contexts. Historical figures bearing this name include Badi' al-Zaman Mirza (died 1514), the last Timurid ruler of Herat, and the medieval writer Badi' al-Zaman al-Hamadani (969-1007), though these represent distinct lineages rather than a single family tree. In modern Gulf contexts, individuals with the Al Badi surname appear in media and other professions, but their specific genealogical connections to historical figures or tribal origins are not documented in the available research.
data.description: Al Badi is a surname found across different historical periods and regions, deriving from the Arabic word meaning 'wonderful' or 'unique', but without established genealogical unity between different bearers of the name.
data.nameEtymology: The name 'Badi' (Arabic: بديع) means 'wonderful,' 'unique,' or 'incomparable' and is one of the 99 Names of God in Islam

data.history: The Al Kalbani family's documented history centers on their migration from Ras Al Khaimah, United Arab Emirates, to Saudi Arabia in the 1950s. Adil al-Kalbani was born in Riyadh on April 4, 1958, to poor emigrants who had settled in the kingdom during this period. His father worked as a government clerk, reflecting the family's modest beginnings in their new homeland. The most prominent member, Adil al-Kalbani, rose from these humble origins to become the first Black imam of the Great Mosque of Mecca, appointed by King Abdullah in 2008 to lead the tarawih prayers during Ramadan. This appointment marked a significant milestone both for the family and for representation in Saudi Arabia's religious hierarchy. Al-Kalbani's career progression from working at Saudi Arabian Airlines while attending evening classes at King Saud University, to becoming a qualified imam in 1994, and eventually serving at the holiest mosque in Islam, demonstrates the family's integration and achievement within Saudi society.
data.description: The Al Kalbani family are Saudi Arabian citizens of Emirati origin, having migrated from Ras Al Khaimah to Saudi Arabia in the 1950s, with their most notable member serving as imam of the Great Mosque of Mecca.
data.originStory: The Al Kalbani family originated from Ras Al Khaimah in the United Arab Emirates, from where they emigrated to Saudi Arabia in the 1950s as poor migrants seeking better opportunities in the kingdom.
data.modernStatus: The family has achieved significant religious prominence in Saudi Arabia, with Adil al-Kalbani serving as imam of Masjid al-Haram and becoming an internationally recognized Islamic scholar and Quranic reciter.
data.nameEtymology: Al-Kalbani is an Arabic surname, though the specific etymology is not provided in the research materials.

data.history: The Al Saharwardi family traces its origins to Baghdad, where they were part of the family of Shaikh Shahabuddin Saharwardi. During the Sama dynasty period, two members of the family - Shaikh Tahar and Shahar Ramadhan - migrated from Baghdad to Sindh on the invitation of the celebrated King Jam Nizamuddin (also known as Jam Nandho). Upon their arrival, Jam Nizamuddin welcomed them and they settled in Patt, a town in Sindh. In recognition of their presence and contributions, the village was officially designated as 'Qabilaul Islam Patt Sharif' in their honor. The family became established in this region, which was already known for its Sufi traditions and religious significance, having been visited by notable figures like Lal Shahbaz Qalandar between 1196-1224.
data.description: The Al Saharwardi family originated from the family of Shaikh Shahabuddin Saharwardi of Baghdad and migrated to Sindh during the Sama dynasty period, settling in Patt Sharif. They were welcomed by King Jam Nizamuddin and the village was renamed in their honor.
data.originStory: During the Sama dynasty, Shaikh Tahar and Shahar Ramadhan from the family of Shaikh Shahabuddin Saharwardi of Baghdad migrated to Sindh on the invitation of King Jam Nizamuddin. They were welcomed and settled in Patt, which was then designated as 'Qabilaul Islam Patt Sharif' in their respect.

data.history: The Khimji Ramdas family is a prominent Indian merchant family that established themselves in Oman as part of the Hindu business community that first arrived in Muscat in 1507 from Kutch. By the early 19th century, there were at least 4,000 Hindus in Oman, all of the intermediate merchant caste, with Khimji Ramdas being among the most prominent immigrant Hindu families alongside Visoomal Damodar Gandhi, Dhanji Morarji, Ratansi Purushottam and Purushottam Toprani. The family founded the Khimji Ramdas Group in 1870, which has grown to operate in infrastructure, consumer products, and retail sectors. Despite the decline of the Hindu community in Oman from thousands in the 19th century to just a few dozen by independence, the Khimji Ramdas family has maintained their prominence and established deep roots in Omani society. The family's commitment to their adopted homeland is demonstrated through their establishment of educational institutions, with the founding of Seth Khimji Ramdas Arya Kelavani Mandal Muttrah School in 1941 (later renamed Indian School Al Wadi Al Kabir), and their continued involvement in Omani civic life. In the modern era, family members like Kanak Khimji and his son Pankaj Khimji have taken leadership roles in sports administration and government advisory positions, with Pankaj being appointed Foreign Trade Advisor to the Omani government by Royal Decree in 2022.
data.description: The Khimji Ramdas family is a prominent Indian merchant family that migrated from Kutch to Oman in the early colonial period, establishing the Khimji Ramdas Group in 1870. They are recognized as one of the most prominent Hindu families in Oman's history, with significant contributions to business, education, and civic life.
data.originStory: The family originated from Kutch and came to Oman as part of the Hindu migration that first arrived in Muscat in 1507, belonging to the intermediate merchant caste community.
data.modernStatus: The family operates the Khimji Ramdas Group established in 1870, active in infrastructure, consumer products, and retail. Current prominent member Pankaj Khimji serves as Foreign Trade Advisor to the Omani government by Royal Decree (2022), chairman of Oman Cricket, Associate Member Director of the ICC, and vice-president of the Asian Cricket Council.

data.history: The Al Wuhaibi family's documented history centers around Sheikh Abd al-ʽAziz al-Wuhaibi's role as a founding member of the Umma Islamic Party, formed on February 10, 2011, during the Arab Spring period in Saudi Arabia. The party was created by a coordination committee of Islamists and intellectuals demanding political reform, representation, and an end to absolute monarchy. On February 17, 2011, Sheikh al-Wuhaibi was arrested along with other party co-founders by Saudi authorities and detained at ʽUlaysha Prison in Riyadh. While his fellow co-founders were conditionally released later in 2011 after signing pledges to cease anti-government activities, Sheikh al-Wuhaibi remained in detention, making him the only founding member who did not sign such declarations.
data.description: The Al Wuhaibi family is known through Sheikh Abd al-ʽAziz al-Wuhaibi, a founding member of Saudi Arabia's pro-reform Umma Islamic Party who was detained in 2011 for political activities.
data.modernStatus: The family's modern status is defined by Sheikh Abd al-ʽAziz al-Wuhaibi's role in Saudi political opposition and his extended detention following the formation of the Umma Islamic Party in 2011.

data.history:
The research provided focuses on Sulaiman Abu Ghaith (full name: Sulaiman Jassem Sulaiman Ali Abu Ghaith), a Kuwaiti national born in 1965 who became one of al-Qaeda's spokesmen. Abu Ghaith grew up with the Muslim Brotherhood and first gained attention during the 1990-1991 Iraqi invasion of Kuwait, where his sermons denouncing the occupation gained him popularity among Kuwaitis. However, the Kuwaiti government later removed him from his mosque and banned him from giving sermons due to his criticism of the government, after which he became a high school religion teacher.

In June 2000, Abu Ghaith left Kuwait for Afghanistan where he met Osama bin Laden and joined al-Qaeda, quickly becoming the organization's spokesman due to his public speaking abilities and comparative youth. He was also a founder of Al Wafa al Igatha al Islamia, a charity that the US asserted provided cover for al-Qaeda's fundraising. After the September 11, 2001 attacks, he appeared in widely circulated videos defending the attacks, which caused Kuwait to strip him of his citizenship.

From 2002 to 2013, Abu Ghaith lived primarily in Iran under house arrest, though he briefly entered Turkey in 2013 before being arrested in Jordan and extradited to the United States. In 2014, he was convicted of conspiring to kill Americans and providing material support to terrorists, receiving a life sentence. He had three wives: first Fatima in Kuwait (six daughters, one son), then Egyptian woman Amal (two daughters), and finally Osama bin Laden's daughter Fatima (one daughter, one son). The research does not provide information about the broader Al Ghaith family's origins, tribal connections, or other family members beyond this individual case.
data.description: Research covers only Sulaiman Abu Ghaith, a Kuwaiti al-Qaeda spokesman, with no information about the broader Al Ghaith family origins or tribal connections.

data.history: The Al Ruwaili family name derives from the Ruwallah (Rwala), a large Arab tribe of the northern Arabian Peninsula and Syrian Desert that came into being in the 16th century as part of the Dhana Maslam branch of the Anizah tribal confederation. The Ruwallah were historically centered in al-Jawf and Sirhan Valley in northern Arabia, with tribal territories extending from al-Qasim in the south to Damascus in the north. Until the early 20th century, they were primarily a warrior tribe active in the region spanning modern-day Saudi Arabia, Jordan, and Syria. The tribe was led by the House of Sha'lan (Al Sha'lan), who developed close ties with the Lebanese government and Saudi royal family. Following the demarcation of Middle Eastern borders in the early 20th century, most tribal members settled into sedentary or urban life across Saudi Arabia, Jordan, and Syria. Today, those retaining tribal appellations use surnames such as Al-Ruwaili to distinguish themselves from their sedentary cousins.
data.description: Al Ruwaili is a family name derived from the Ruwallah tribe, part of the larger Anizah confederation, historically centered in northern Arabia. Modern members have settled across the Gulf region and hold prominent positions in military and other sectors.
data.originStory: The Ruwallah tribe came into being sometime in the 16th century or shortly thereafter as part of the Dhana Maslam branch of the large Anizah tribal confederation. They were historically an almost entirely warrior tribe centered in the region of al-Jawf and Sirhan Valley in northern Arabia.
data.modernStatus: Contemporary Al Ruwaili family members include high-ranking military officers such as Air Chief Marshal Fayyadh bin Hamed bin Raqed Al Ruwaili, who has served as Chairman of the General Staff of the Saudi Arabian Armed Forces since 2018, and professional footballers like Omar Al-Ruwaili. The family maintains connections across Saudi Arabia, Jordan, and Syria.
data.nameEtymology: Al Ruwaili derives from the Ruwallah (Arabic: الرولة, Rwala Arabic ir-Rwāle), with the singular form being Ruweili/Ruwaili, referring to members of this large Arab tribe.

data.history: The Al Subaie family appears in historical records through marriage connections to prominent Saudi and Gulf families from the 20th century onwards. Based on available documentation, family members have been connected to the Saudi royal family and other prominent Gulf families through matrimonial alliances spanning several decades. Buniah bint Musl'am bin Harqan Al Subaie was married to Prince Faisal bin Turki I Al Saud (1918-1968), who served as Saudi Arabia's first minister of labour and social affairs and later as minister of interior. Additionally, King Fahd bin Abdulaziz Al Saud was married to Joza'a bint Sultan Al Adgham Al Subaie, though this marriage ended in divorce. Prince Bandar bin Abdulaziz Al Saud also married Munira bint Muhammad Al Majrur Al Jabour Al Subaie as one of his three wives. These matrimonial connections suggest the Al Subaie family held significant social standing within Gulf society, particularly in Saudi Arabia, during the mid-20th century period of the kingdom's modern development.
data.description: A Gulf family documented through marriage connections to the Saudi royal family and other prominent families during the 20th century, with members holding significant social positions.
data.modernStatus: Current status unclear from available records, though historical connections suggest continued presence in Gulf society

data.history: Al Hadba is primarily known as a political party formed to contest the 2009 Iraqi governorate elections in Ninawa province, mostly comprised of Sunni Arabs. The party was led by Atheel al-Nujaifi, brother of Osama al-Nujaifi who was part of the Iraqi National List. According to preliminary results, the Hadba party received more than 48% of the vote in Ninawa province, making it perhaps the most successful new political party in those elections. The party campaigned as the voice of Mosul's Arab majority, promising to support a strong Iraqi central government and oppose annexation of northern Ninawa province by the Kurdistan Regional Government. The name 'al-Hadba' references the leaning minaret of the oldest mosque in Mosul, the Hadbaa' Minaret built more than 800 years ago by Sultan Nour Al-Din, which has been likened to the leaning tower of Pisa and is considered a symbol of Mosul's historical identity. The party sought to form political alliances to govern the province without depending on Kurdish votes, and announced alliances with smaller parties including the Yazidi Reformist Progressive Front and the Shabak Democratic Assembly.
data.description: Al Hadba is a Sunni Arab political party formed for the 2009 Iraqi elections in Ninawa province, led by the al-Nujaifi family and named after Mosul's historic leaning minaret.
data.modernStatus: Political party that achieved significant success in 2009 Ninawa provincial elections with over 48% of votes, led by prominent Mosul family the al-Nujafis who are known as businessmen and breeders of prize Arabian horses

data.history: The Al Daham family appears in the research primarily through individual members rather than as a distinct family entity. The most prominent reference is to Daham bin Dawas al-Shalaan, the 18th-century ruler of Riyadh who built the first fortifications around the city in the 1740s and constructed what would become Qasr al-Hukm palace in 1747. He abandoned the palace and fled Riyadh for Bani Khalid-ruled al-Hasa when Muhammad ibn Saud's forces advanced towards the city in 1773. The research also mentions Daham Abdul al-Rashid, who served as head of the National Audit Bureau and was part of the Abdul al-Rashid family, members of the al-Bu Nasir tribe who wielded considerable power in Saddam Hussein's regime. Additionally, there are references to individuals named Daham in various contexts, including Noureddine Daham (born 1977), an Algerian footballer, and Daham Miro (1921-2010), a Kurdish political leader, though these appear to be unrelated individuals rather than members of a cohesive Al Daham family lineage.
data.description: The Al Daham name appears across various historical contexts, most notably through Daham bin Dawas al-Shalaan, the 18th-century ruler of Riyadh who built the city's first fortifications and palace. Other individuals with the Daham name appear in different regions and time periods without clear family connections.

data.history: The Al Mishal family represents a branch of the Al Sabah ruling family of Kuwait, tracing their lineage directly to Ahmad Al-Jaber Al-Sabah, who ruled Kuwait from 1921 to 1950. The family's most prominent member is Mishal Al-Ahmad Al-Jaber Al-Sabah, born in 1940 as the seventh son of Ahmad Al-Jaber Al-Sabah. Mishal built his career in Kuwait's security apparatus, graduating from Hendon Police College in 1960 and serving as head of the Ministry of Interior's intelligence service from 1967 to 1980, overseeing its development into the Kuwait State Security service. He later served as deputy chief of the Kuwait National Guard from 2004 to 2020, implementing significant reforms and anti-corruption measures. Following the death of his half-brother Nawaf Al-Ahmad Al-Jaber Al-Sabah in December 2023, Mishal ascended to become Emir of Kuwait, marking the continuation of the Al Sabah dynasty's rule.
data.description: The Al Mishal family is a branch of Kuwait's ruling Al Sabah family, with Mishal Al-Ahmad Al-Jaber Al-Sabah serving as the current Emir of Kuwait since 2023. The family has been deeply involved in Kuwait's security and defense establishment.
data.originStory: The Al Mishal lineage stems directly from Ahmad Al-Jaber Al-Sabah, the tenth ruler of Kuwait (1921-1950), making them part of the broader Al Sabah ruling family that has governed Kuwait since the 18th century.
data.modernStatus: Currently holds the highest position in Kuwait with Mishal Al-Ahmad serving as Emir of Kuwait since December 2023. The family maintains significant influence in Kuwait's security establishment and government affairs.

data.history: Based on the research provided, the Al Saeed family appears to encompass multiple distinct lineages across the GCC region, with no confirmed common origin. The most prominent branch documented is the Al Maktoum ruling family of Dubai, where Sheikh Saeed bin Maktoum bin Hasher Al Maktoum (1878-1958) served as Dubai's longest-ruling leader from 1912 to 1958. This branch traces to Maktoum bin Hasher, who ruled Dubai from 1894-1906, establishing a dynasty that continues today through his descendants including current ruler Sheikh Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum. Sheikh Saeed's reign encompassed the collapse of the pearling industry, Dubai's transformation into a trading hub, and the foundation of the modern UAE, with his son Rashid bin Saeed Al Maktoum becoming a founding father of the Emirates. The research also references other Al Saeed lineages, including the Al Said royal family of Oman (as seen with Meyyan bint Shihab Al Said), suggesting the name represents multiple unrelated families rather than a single tribal origin. These various Al Saeed branches appear to have developed independently across different emirates and regions.
data.description: Al Saeed encompasses multiple distinct family lineages across the GCC, most notably including the Al Maktoum ruling dynasty of Dubai descended from Sheikh Saeed bin Maktoum Al Maktoum (1878-1958), with no confirmed common tribal origin between different branches.
data.modernStatus: The Al Maktoum branch of Al Saeed continues as the ruling family of Dubai, with Sheikh Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum currently serving as ruler. Other documented Al Saeed branches include connections to Omani royalty and various business families across the region, though their specific current status varies.
data.nameEtymology: The name 'Saeed' derives from Arabic meaning 'blessed, lucky, joy' or 'happy, patient', stemming from the Arabic verb sa'ada meaning 'to be happy, fortunate or lucky'.

data.history: The Al Yabhouni family, represented in modern times by the Al Yabhouni Al Daheri branch, has established itself as a prominent force in the UAE's entertainment and media industry. The most notable contemporary figure is Mansoor Al Yabhouni Al Daheri, born on January 14, 1975, in Abu Dhabi, who has become a significant entrepreneur and film producer. Al Daheri founded Al Kalema Production in January 2012 and Film Gate Production in January 2018, creating companies that have produced numerous films, TV series, and documentaries reflecting Emirati culture. His educational background includes an MBA from the New York Institute of Technology (2007-2009) and an MFA in Filmmaking from the New York Film Academy (2010-2012), along with advanced project management certification from Stanford University. His productions have gained international recognition, appearing at prestigious festivals including Cannes Film Festival, Dubai International Film Festival, and Abu Dhabi Film Festival, establishing the Al Yabhouni name in global entertainment circles.
data.description: The Al Yabhouni family is represented by contemporary media entrepreneur Mansoor Al Yabhouni Al Daheri, a prominent Emirati film producer who founded multiple production companies and has achieved international recognition in the entertainment industry. The family has established itself in the UAE's modern cultural and business landscape through significant contributions to filmmaking and media production.

data.history: The Al Junaibi family is documented through several notable modern members, particularly in sports, military, and media sectors. The family includes prominent footballer Ismail Matar Ibrahim Khamis Al Mukhaini Al Junaibi, who achieved international recognition winning the Golden Ball at the 2003 World Youth Championships and leading the UAE to their first Gulf Cup trophy in 2007. The Al Mukhaini lineage within the family traces origins to Sur city in Oman, as documented through Ismail Matar's genealogy. The family has produced multiple generations involved in UAE football, with Ismail Matar having two footballer brothers, Ahmed (retired) and Yasser (born 1987), indicating an established presence in UAE sports culture. Military service is also represented through Major General Khalifa bin Abdullah Al Junaibi, who received the Military Order of Achievement First Class in 2015, and Brigadier General Muslim bin Salem Al Junaibi, who received the Second Class in the same year. The family's contemporary prominence spans multiple Emirates, with members holding significant positions in sports, military, and media organizations including Abu Dhabi Media Network.

data.modernStatus: Contemporary Al Junaibi family members hold prominent positions across UAE institutions, including international sports representation, senior military commands with recipients of national honors, and leadership roles in major media organizations.

data.history: The Na'im (singular Al Nuaimi) is a traditionally Ghafiri Arab tribe originating in the United Arab Emirates, with presence across other Gulf countries including Qatar, Bahrain, and Oman. By the early 20th century, the tribe numbered approximately 13,000 according to the Gazetteer of the Persian Gulf, though their population and political influence had been declining for decades due to internal divisions. The tribe is divided into three main sections: the Al Bu Kharaiban (from which the current Rulers of Ajman are drawn), the Khawatir, and the Al Bu Shamis. The traditional heart of Na'im territory was the oasis town of Buraimi and nearby Al Ain, where they expanded at the expense of the Dhawahir tribe. The Na'im followed the Ghafiri tradition and supported the Omani Imam Yarub in his bid to control Oman in 1723. Although initially linked to Wahhabi influence in the Buraimi area, they later allied with other forces to evict the Wahhabis from Buraimi in 1848 and 1869, subsequently occupying many forts around the area. In Qatar, the tribe was reported as one of the most powerful in an 1890 British report, though they later split into factions during disputes over territorial control.
data.description: The Na'im (Al Nuaimi) is a traditionally Ghafiri Arab tribe based primarily in the UAE, particularly around Buraimi and Al Ain, with significant populations across the Gulf. The tribe is divided into three main sections and has produced the ruling family of Ajman emirate.
data.modernStatus: The Al Bu Kharaiban section of the Na'im tribe provides the current ruling family of the Emirate of Ajman in the UAE. The tribe maintains presence across multiple Gulf states with various members holding significant political, academic, and business positions.

data.history: The Al Maliki family represents multiple distinct lineages across the Gulf and Arab world, with no single unified origin. The most prominent political branch is represented by Nouri al-Maliki, born in 1950 in Janaja village, Abu Gharaq, central Iraq. He belongs to the Al-Ali Tribe, described as an offshoot of the Bani Malik tribe. His grandfather Muhammad Hasan Abi al-Mahasin was a poet and cleric who served as Iraq's Minister of Education under King Faisal I and represented the Revolutionary Council during the 1920 Iraqi revolution against the British. A separate Saudi Arabian Al Maliki lineage produced the renowned Islamic scholar Muhammad ibn Alawi al-Maliki (1944-2004) in Mecca, whose family had taught in the Haram of Mecca for centuries with five ancestors serving as Maliki Imams of the Sacred Mosque. This Meccan family gained prominence through religious scholarship rather than tribal politics. A third branch includes the contemporary Saudi religious reformist Hassan Farhan al-Maliki, arrested in 2017 for his liberal Islamic views, though his specific family connections remain unclear from available sources.
data.description: The Al Maliki represents multiple unrelated family lineages, including Iraqi politicians from the Al-Ali/Bani Malik tribal background, Meccan religious scholars with centuries-old ties to the Sacred Mosque, and Saudi religious reformists.
data.originStory: The Iraqi Al Maliki branch traces to the Al-Ali Tribe, an offshoot of the Bani Malik tribe in central Iraq. The Meccan Al Maliki family has taught in the Haram of Mecca for centuries, with five ancestors serving as Maliki Imams of the Sacred Mosque.
data.modernStatus: The Iraqi branch gained major political prominence through Nouri al-Maliki's service as Prime Minister of Iraq (2006-2014) and Vice President (2014-2015, 2016-2018), leading the Islamic Dawa Party. The Saudi Meccan branch maintained religious scholarly influence until the death of Muhammad ibn Alawi al-Maliki in 2004.

data.history: The Al Ajman family is not a distinct ruling family, but rather refers to the Al Nuaimi family that has ruled the Emirate of Ajman since 1816. The ruling family belongs to the Al Bu Kharaiban section of the Na'im tribal confederation. In 1816, Sheikh Rashid bin Humaid Al Nuaimi and fifty followers conquered the coastal settlement of Ajman from the Al Bu Shamis Nuaimi tribe in a brief conflict. The Ajman fort fell to Rashid's forces in 1816-1817, with his rule endorsed by Sheikh Sultan bin Saqr Al Qasimi of Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah. The family signed the General Maritime Treaty with Britain in 1820, followed by subsequent treaties in 1835, 1843, 1853, and 1892 that bound the Trucial States to Britain. The Al Nuaimi family has maintained continuous rule of Ajman through various political changes, including the formation of the United Arab Emirates in 1971 under Sheikh Rashid bin Humayd Al Nuaimi, and continues under the current ruler Sheikh Humaid bin Rashid Al Nuaimi III since 1981.
data.description: The Al Nuaimi family has been the ruling royal family of Ajman emirate since 1816, belonging to the Al Bu Kharaiban section of the Na'im tribal confederation. They established control through conquest and have maintained continuous rule for over two centuries.
data.originStory: In 1816, Sheikh Rashid bin Humaid Al Nuaimi led fifty followers to conquer the coastal settlement of Ajman from the Al Bu Shamis Nuaimi tribe through military conflict, establishing Al Bu Kharaiban Nuaimi rule that continues today.
data.modernStatus: Currently rules the Emirate of Ajman under Sheikh Humaid bin Rashid Al Nuaimi III since 1981, with Crown Prince Sheikh Ammar bin Humaid Al Nuaimi as heir apparent. The family oversees Ajman's government, economy, and contributes four seats to the UAE Federal National Council.
data.nameEtymology: The word Ajman comes from Arabic عَجْمان (ʕajmān), related to عَجَم (ʕajam, 'foreigner'), because the area was at one time inhabited by Persians.

data.history: The Al Rashid Al Maktoum family are members of the Al Bu Falasah tribe and rulers of Dubai. Sheikh Rashid bin Saeed Al Maktoum became ruler of Dubai in 1958 upon his father's death, having effectively managed emirate affairs since the late 1930s. He was responsible for transforming Dubai from a small creek settlement into a modern port city and regional commercial hub through visionary infrastructure projects including creek dredging (1958-1959), Port Rashid construction, and later Jebel Ali Port development. The family played a crucial role in UAE formation, with Rashid meeting Abu Dhabi's Sheikh Zayed in 1968 at Argoub Al Sedirah to establish the union through a historic handshake agreement. Upon Rashid's death in 1990, his eldest son Maktoum became ruler and served as UAE Prime Minister until 2006, when he was succeeded by his brother Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum, the current ruler of Dubai and UAE Vice President and Prime Minister.
data.description: The ruling family of Dubai, members of the Al Bu Falasah tribe, who transformed the emirate from a trading port into a modern global city and played a founding role in the United Arab Emirates.
data.originStory: The family belongs to the Al Bu Falasah tribe and has ruled Dubai through the Al Maktoum lineage, with Sheikh Saeed bin Maktoum Al Maktoum ruling until 1958 when succeeded by his son Rashid.
data.modernStatus: Currently rules Dubai with Sheikh Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum as ruler, UAE Vice President and Prime Minister. The family controls major business interests including Dubai Holding, Emirates airline, and Godolphin horse racing stables.

data.history: The Al Jasmi family appears to be an Emirati family with documented presence in the UAE, particularly in the emirates of Sharjah and Abu Dhabi. The family has produced notable figures in entertainment, sports, and beauty pageants. Hussain Al Jassmi, born in 1979 in Khorfakkan, Sharjah, to an Emirati father and Egyptian mother, became a prominent singer known throughout the Arab world. His career began with singles like 'Bawada'ak' and 'Wallah Mayiswa', and he gained international recognition performing at venues like the Vatican's annual Christmas concert and Dubai's Expo 2020 opening ceremony. The family faced tragedy in 2024-2025 with the deaths of Hussain's brothers Jamal and Hasan. Other notable family members include swimmer Obaid Al-Jasmi from Abu Dhabi, who represented the UAE at the 2004 and 2008 Olympics, and beauty queen Noura Al Jasmi, who became Miss Earth UAE 2024 at age 23.
data.description: An Emirati family with documented presence in Sharjah and Abu Dhabi, known for producing prominent figures in entertainment, sports, and beauty pageants.
data.modernStatus: The family maintains public prominence through entertainment and sports, with members achieving international recognition in their respective fields.

data.history: The Al Musa family referenced in this research appears to be the Al-Sadr family, a Hashemite Arab clerical Shia family originating from Jabal Amel in Lebanon. They are a branch of the Musawi family tracing to Musa Ibn Jaafar, the seventh Shia Imam. The family's documented lineage begins with Sayyid Saleh Moussawi Ameli, a descendant of Imam Musa Al Kadhim through 27 generations. The Al-Sadr branch specifically derives from the Charafeddine family of Jabal Amel, with their ancestor S. Salih b. Muhammad Sharafeddin being born in Shhour, a village near Tyre, Lebanon. Following anti-Ottoman uprising events, Salih left for Najaf, and his lineage subsequently moved between Lebanon, Iraq, and Iran. The family has produced numerous prominent Islamic scholars across these three countries. Most notably, Musa al-Sadr (1928-1978) became a towering figure in Lebanese Shia politics, founding the Amal Movement and serving as the first head of the Supreme Islamic Shia Council in Lebanon before his mysterious disappearance in Libya in 1978. The family's influence extends across religious scholarship, with members like Mohammad Baqir al-Sadr becoming major Islamic thinkers, while others like Muqtada al-Sadr have wielded significant political power in Iraq.
data.description: Al Musa refers to the Al-Sadr family, a Hashemite Arab clerical Shia family from Jabal Amel, Lebanon, representing a branch of the Musawi lineage tracing to the seventh Shia Imam. The family has produced influential Islamic scholars and political leaders across Lebanon, Iraq, and Iran over several centuries.
data.originStory: The family traces its origins to Jabal Amel in Lebanon through the Charafeddine family lineage. Their ancestor S. Salih b. Muhammad Sharafeddin was born in Shhour village near Tyre, Lebanon, but was forced to leave for Najaf following events related to an anti-Ottoman uprising. The family claims descent from Musa Ibn Jaafar, the seventh Shia Imam, through 27 documented generations.
data.modernStatus: The family continues to hold significant religious and political influence across the Middle East, with members leading major movements like Amal in Lebanon and the Sadrist Movement in Iraq, while maintaining important clerical positions in Shia religious institutions.
data.nameEtymology: Al Musa derives from Musa Ibn Jaafar (Musa Al Kadhim), the seventh Shia Imam, from whom the Musawi family claims descent. The name Musa is the Arabic form of Moses.

data.history: Based on the research provided, there is a connection between the Al Suwaid family and Sheikh Abd al-Zahra al-Suwaid, who was identified as a follower of the Green Party and supporter of Muqtada al-Sadr during a major demonstration at Firdos Square in Baghdad on April 9, 2005. Sheikh Abd al-Zahra al-Suwaid was quoted as stating to the gathered protesters: 'The rally must be peaceful. You should demand the withdrawal of the occupation forces and press for quicker trials for Saddam Hussein and his aides before an Iraqi court.' This demonstration occurred on the second anniversary of the Iraq invasion and involved tens of thousands of Iraqis protesting the American occupation. The presence of a prominent Al-Suwaid figure in this significant political event suggests the family has maintained political influence in Iraq during the modern era. However, the research does not provide comprehensive information about the family's origins, historical development, or broader genealogical connections beyond this single documented political involvement.

data.history: The Al Bu Mahair were one of three tribal groups, along with the Mazari and Manasir tribes, that formed the settlement of Al Khan around the start of the 20th century. The settlement consisted of approximately 75 pearling boats and was administered by a tribal ruler. Al Khan was located on the bay between Sharjah and Dubai, serving as an important pearling community during this period. The area experienced political tensions when conflicts arose in 1917 between the ruler of Sharjah and the local headman Muhammad bin Ubaid bin Jarash over absconding debtors from other pearling communities who had taken refuge in the township. These uneasy relations between Al Khan and Sharjah continued until after 1934, when the Ruler of Sharjah appointed his brother Mohammad as Wali (governor) over the township, bringing it under more direct Sharjah control. The settlement was also affected by broader political conflicts, including a firefight in February 1940 between leaders of the rebellious Majlis movement and forces loyal to the Ruler of Dubai, which required mediation by multiple rulers to resolve.
data.description: Al Bu Mahair was one of three tribes that settled in Al Khan, a pearling community of around 75 boats located on the bay between Sharjah and Dubai in the early 20th century. The tribe was part of a settlement that came under Sharjah control after 1934.

data.history: Al Mahmoud is documented as a Sunni Arab family based in Qatar and the UAE, originating from the Banu Tamim tribe and specifically from the Al Maʿāḍīd branch, alongside the Al Thani family. The family migrated from Najd, specifically from the Hotat Bani Tamim area in central Arabia. However, the available research provides extremely limited information about this family's specific history, migration timeline, or notable achievements. The sparse documentation suggests they share tribal origins with Qatar's ruling Al Thani family, indicating potential historical significance in the region's tribal structure, but detailed historical records of their settlement patterns, economic activities, or political roles are not available in the current research.

data.history: The Al Khalidi (Arabic: الْخَالْدِي) are descendants of the Bani Khalid tribe, which traditionally claims descent from Khalid ibn al-Walid, a senior companion of the Prophet Muhammad and esteemed general crucial in the Islamic conquest of Persia and Syria, though this claim has been questioned by Arab genealogists. The Bani Khalid historically dominated the deserts surrounding Al-Hasa and Al-Qatif during the 15th-18th centuries, with the chieftainship traditionally held by the Al Humaid clan from the Banu Jabr section. Under Barrak ibn Ghurayr of the Al Humaid, they expelled Ottoman garrisons and Portuguese forces from Eastern Arabia in 1670, establishing their capital in Al-Mubarraz near Al-Ahsa. The Bani Khalid maintained power until they fell to the Saudi forces in 1793 when Al 'Ura'yir was deposed from al-Hasa. After the Ottomans overthrew Al Saud in 1818, they reinstated members of Al 'Uray'ir as rulers, but the Bani Khalid were eventually defeated by tribal alliances and Al Saud forces in battles in 1823 and 1830, ending their rule permanently. Many Khalidis subsequently scattered to Iraq, the Levant, and other regions across the Arabian Peninsula and beyond. The Khalidis of Jerusalem represent a distinct branch that rose to prominence during Mamluk rule and became one of the most powerful families in Palestine, holding key Ottoman offices and producing notable mayors, politicians, and intellectuals who opposed early Zionism.
data.description: Al Khalidi is a family name given to descendants of the Bani Khalid tribe, which historically ruled Eastern Arabia from 1670-1830 before scattering across the Middle East. Today they are found throughout Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, Qatar, Oman, Iraq, Jordan, Syria, Palestine, Lebanon, and the UAE.
data.originStory: The Bani Khalid traditionally claims descent from Khalid ibn al-Walid, the renowned Islamic military commander known as 'Sayf Allah al-Maslul' (The Drawn Sword of Allah), who played a crucial role in the Islamic conquests of Persia and Syria. However, this genealogical claim has been questioned by Arab genealogists, making the actual origins disputed.
data.modernStatus: Al Khalidi descendants today live across multiple countries with notable members including academics like Walid Khalidi (Oxford University professor), Rashid Khalidi (Columbia University professor), and various business and political figures. The Jerusalem branch maintains the historic Khalidi Library near Al-Aqsa Mosque and has produced influential Palestinian intellectuals and historians.

folkLegends.title: Protectors of the Habari
folkLegends.story: According to Arabian folklore, one chief of the Bani Khalid attempted to protect the prized desert bustard (Habari) from extinction by prohibiting the bedouin in his realm from poaching the bird's eggs, earning the tribe the appellation of 'protectors of the eggs of the Habari', an allusion to the chief's absolute supremacy over his realm.

data.nameEtymology: Al-Khaldi (الْخَالْدِي) is derived from the tribal name Bani Khalid, which in turn claims connection to Khalid ibn al-Walid, the famous Islamic military commander.

migrationPath.description: After defeat by Saudi forces and final loss of power, many Khalidis scattered to Iraq, Syria, Jordan, Palestine and Lebanon

data.history: Based on the available research, the Al Mahmud family name derives from the Arabic root meaning 'praise,' shared with the name Muhammad. The research provides extensive historical context about Mahmud of Ghazni (971-1030 CE), a prominent historical figure who ruled the Ghaznavid Empire from 998 to 1030, though no direct connection to any modern Gulf Al Mahmud family is established in the sources. The name Mahmud and its variants (Mahmoud, Mahmood) appear across the Islamic world in various forms, suggesting multiple independent origins rather than a single family lineage. Without specific genealogical documentation connecting any Gulf-based Al Mahmud family to these historical figures or establishing their particular tribal origins, migration patterns, or settlement history in the GCC region, no definitive family history can be constructed from the available sources.
data.description: A family name derived from the Arabic root meaning 'praise,' common throughout the Islamic world, with no confirmed specific origin or tribal affiliation documented in available research.
data.nameEtymology: Derived from the Arabic triconsonantal root Ḥ-M-D, meaning praise, along with Muhammad
